entries.prompt: Walk me through a moment from the last few days that's still replaying in your head — what happened, what you said or didn't say, and what you keep coming back to about it.
entries.content:
Tuesday, picking Nina up from the thing at the rec center, and another mom — I think her name is Karen but I'm not going to commit to that — leaned in through the passenger window while Nina was still gathering her stuff and asked, in that tone, *so what are you up to these days?* And I gave her the answer I have been giving people, which is some version of "I'm building out a coaching practice, working with a few people, figuring out the shape of it," and she nodded the way people nod when they have already decided what your answer means, and said *good for you, that sounds like a nice break,* and then Nina got in the car and we drove home.

That's it. That's the whole thing. And it has been sitting on top of my chest for three days.

It's not what she said. *A nice break* is not even unkind. It's probably what I would have thought from the outside about somebody like me a year ago — *oh, she's taking some time.* The sting is that I cannot prove her wrong. I have nothing to point to. I don't have a website that would shut her up. I don't have a roster. I have four conversations a week with people who are mostly being generous to me, and a Google doc that I keep rewriting the top of, and a calendar that is so empty that when I block time to think it feels like I am performing for an audience of one.

What I keep coming back to is that I had a sentence ready and I didn't use it. I had been rehearsing — embarrassing to admit but true — a version where I say *actually it's going well, I have X clients and we're doing Y,* something with shape and edges. And the moment came and I went generic. I went *figuring out the shape of it.* Which is honest, I guess. But honest in a way that I don't think I would have allowed when I was thirty-five. At thirty-five I would have made it sound like a thing. Now I can't tell if I have lost the knack or if I have just stopped being willing to do that little dance, and I don't know which would be worse.

And underneath that — I think the reason it stuck — is that I was, for half a second, embarrassed in front of my eleven-year-old. Nina was buckling in. She probably wasn't listening. But she could have been. And what she would have heard is some woman at a window deciding her mom is on a break. Nina, who at dinner two months ago asked me *what is your job now,* and I said *I help people figure out what they want to do next,* and she said *like a guidance counselor?* and I said *kind of, for grown-ups,* and she said *huh,* and went back to her pasta. I don't think Nina is judging me. I think Nina is eleven and busy. But I keep imagining her at twenty-five being asked about her mother and saying *yeah she did this thing where she quit her job and tried to figure out something new and it sort of —* and the sentence ends in a shrug.

That's the voice. That's the one that's loud right now. *You stepped off, and now you're going to be a person who stepped off.* My mother called Wednesday. She always calls on Wednesdays. She asked how the work was going and I said fine, busy enough, and she said *well, I'm sure something will come together soon,* which she means kindly and which lands like a small stone every time. *Come together.* As though it's weather.

What I am trying to do — and I notice I am bad at this — is sit with the fact that the rec center mom was reading the situation correctly in some way. From the outside I am a 47-year-old woman in a parking lot in the middle of a Tuesday afternoon picking up her kid. She is not wrong about what she sees. She is wrong about what it means, but she is not wrong about what she sees. The gap between those two things is the entire problem, and the whole reason I left was that I had stopped believing the outside version was telling the truth about my life. So why do I want her to think well of me. Why am I still running the math on what someone in a Subaru thinks of me at 4:15 in the afternoon.

Jamie, when I told the story at dinner, mostly laughed and said *Karen is also mostly figuring out the shape of things, I bet.* Which was the right answer and also not enough. I wanted Jamie to be more outraged on my behalf. I wanted Jamie to say *that woman is an idiot, you are doing something brave.* Which is unfair to ask of Jamie, who has been doing the dishes more often than I have for nine months, and who said *you have permission* and who I think actually meant it but who is also tired in a way that I notice when Jamie sits down at night and doesn't get up again.

The thing I want to write down so I see it tomorrow morning: the sentence I didn't say wouldn't have been true yet. If I had said *I have ten clients and a clear offer,* she would have nodded the same way and I would have walked into the kitchen knowing I lied to a near-stranger to feel okay for thirty seconds. The bad answer was the truthful one. That is not nothing. That might even be the point. But God, it would be easier to live in the bad answer if I had something to hand her along with it. *Figuring out the shape of it, here's a card.* A card would help. I could make a card.

I'm not going to make a card today. I'm going to walk the dog.

entries.prompt: When you walked back to the car after that conversation, what did you actually want to have said to her? Write the version of the answer you didn't give — and then tell me why you gave the one you did.
entries.content:
The dog has burrs in her chest fur from yesterday's walk and I have been picking them out at the kitchen table for the last twenty minutes, which is the kind of task that lets the actual question sit there next to me without me having to look at it directly. So. The version I didn't give.

The version I didn't give would have started with *actually,* which is already a tell, because *actually* is what you say when you are correcting someone who hasn't asked to be corrected. *Actually, I'm working with a small group of people one-on-one, helping them think through career transitions, and it's the most interesting work I have done in years.* That sentence is not a lie. The number of people is small enough that I am embarrassed to name it but it isn't zero. The word *interesting* is doing a lot of lifting but it is doing honest lifting; the conversations are interesting, in a way the old job had stopped being. *The most interesting work I have done in years* — that part I would have meant. I think I would have meant it.

And then the second sentence, the one that would have done the work of shutting the window: *I left a twenty-year career to do this on purpose, and I am taking the time it takes to build it right.* On purpose. That's the phrase I wanted in the air. Not *I'm on a break.* Not *figuring out the shape of it,* which sounds, hearing it back, like I am describing a piece of pottery slumping on the wheel. *On purpose.* As though purpose were a thing you could put on a sentence like a hat.

I think there's a third sentence I would have wanted, and this is the one that is harder to admit to. Something like *and I think a year from now you'll see what I mean.* A promissory note. A please-suspend-judgment-with-interest-accruing. Which is the sentence of someone who needs the rec center mom to come back in twelve months and find me vindicated, which is, I notice, a slightly insane thing to need from a woman whose name might not even be Karen.

So that's the version. *Actually, I'm working with a small group of people, it's the most interesting work I've done in years, I left on purpose, and I think a year from now you'll see what I mean.* Said pleasantly, with a smile, while my eleven-year-old buckles her seatbelt. Mic drop in the rec center parking lot.

Why I didn't say it.

The first reason is the easy one and probably the least true: I didn't have it ready in my mouth. The rehearsals were for a different conversation, the kind of conversation where someone earnest asks a real question and I get to give a real answer. Karen-or-not-Karen wasn't asking. She was making conversation through a car window. The script I had prepared was for an audience that wasn't in the room. I had a soliloquy and she handed me a greeting card.

The second reason is that I could feel, the second she said *so what are you up to these days,* that any answer with shape was going to sound like I was protesting. *I'm doing this and this and this* in response to a casual hello reads, from outside, as a person who really wants you to know they are not on a break. And the more vehement, the more on-a-break it sounds. There is no version of the good answer that doesn't curdle into the same bad answer when delivered with too much wattage at 4:15 on a Tuesday. I knew that in my body before I knew it in my head.

The third reason is the one I have been turning over since Tuesday. I think I didn't say the good version because I was not, in that moment, willing to sell it. Not couldn't — wouldn't. There is a kind of selling I used to do, fluently, in the old career, where you take the most flattering true thing about your situation and you put it forward and you let the person draw a generous picture from it, and it is not lying, it is just curating. I was good at it. I think I was *known* for it, in some small way. And somewhere in the last nine months — I cannot tell you when — I lost the appetite for it. Not the ability. The appetite. When I tried to summon the version with shape and edges, some part of me said *no, not for her, not for this,* and went generic instead. *Figuring out the shape of it.* Which is the truthful answer offered without any effort to dress it up, and offered, I think, almost a little defiantly. Take it or don't.

The trouble is I cannot tell whether that's growth or whether it's the beginning of something I will regret. The thirty-five-year-old version of me would have done the curating without thinking and felt fine afterward. The forty-seven-year-old version did not curate and has been chewing on it for three days. One of those people is going to be more right than the other in five years and I do not know which. I want it to be the forty-seven-year-old. I am not sure.

What I keep landing on, though, is that the answer I would have given is an answer I want to be true, and the answer I gave is an answer that is true. And maybe the work right now, the actual work, not the Google doc work, is to close the gap between those two — not by getting better at the giving, but by making the having be more solid. Until then I am going to keep going generic in parking lots, and I am going to keep being annoyed at myself about it, and I am going to keep picking burrs out of the dog.

Jamie just came in and asked if I want more coffee. I do.

entries.prompt: Think about something small you spent money on this week — a coffee, a thing from the internet, a meal out. Walk me through the moment of buying it. What were you telling yourself in the seconds before, and how did it sit a few hours after?
entries.content:
The boots. I bought boots on Sunday night and I want to write about the boots.

Not expensive boots, by the standards of boots. Not cheap either. Brown leather, the kind with a real sole, the kind that will last if I take care of them, which is part of what the website wanted me to feel and which I went ahead and felt. I had been looking at them, on and off, for about six weeks. Open the tab, close the tab. Open the tab on a Sunday night around ten when the kids are upstairs and Jamie is reading and the dog is on her side breathing the deep breath, and there they were again, and I clicked the size and I clicked the color and I clicked the button and they will be here Thursday.

What I was telling myself in the seconds before. This is the part I want to be honest about because the seconds before were doing a lot of negotiating.

The first thing I told myself was that my old boots — the ones I have been wearing for walks every morning since October — are actually falling apart, which is sort of true. The sole on the left one has a crack across the ball of the foot and I can feel it on wet grass. So there was a real reason. There is always a real reason. The real reason is what you put on top of the other reasons so you can get to the button.

The second thing I told myself, which I was less proud of, was that I deserved them. I am not sure where that word comes from in my head — *deserve* — but it shows up around purchases, and it showed up Sunday. *You have been working. You have been doing the thing. You have been getting up early and walking the dog and trying. The boots are fine.* Which is the kind of sentence that, if I heard it out of someone else's mouth about their own purchase, I would clock immediately as the sentence of a person trying to convert effort into permission. There has been no income for nine months. The math on *deserve* is a little different now. I know this. I bought them anyway.

The third thing — and this is the one I noticed only after I'd clicked — was something like *these are the boots of a person who is doing fine.* A person who is doing fine buys good boots in the fall. A person who is doing fine does not flinch at the number. A person who is doing fine wears them on the morning walk and looks, to anyone passing, like a person whose life is on the rails. I did not articulate this to myself in those words at ten p.m. on Sunday. But I can feel, looking back, that the boots were doing some of the work of the sentence I didn't say in the parking lot. Not a card. Boots.

A few hours after — well, by which I mean the next morning, because I went to bed pretty soon after — there was a small bad feeling that I want to describe accurately. It wasn't regret, exactly. It was the feeling of having done something slightly out of register with the rest of the picture. We are not in trouble. The spreadsheet I look at too late at night still says what it has been saying, which is that we are okay if we are careful, and the boots do not change the spreadsheet in any meaningful way. But *careful* is doing a lot of work in that sentence, and the boots were not, by any honest measure, careful. They were a small unilateral decision made in a tab at ten p.m. about what kind of person I am and what I get to have.

I haven't told Jamie. That is also part of this. We don't have a rule about telling each other when we buy things; we are adults with a shared account and a long marriage and a general sense of each other's spending. But I noticed Monday morning, when Jamie was making lunches, that I did not mention the boots, and I noticed that I noticed. If I had bought them in March, when I was still drawing a paycheck, I would have said *oh by the way I finally pulled the trigger on those boots* and Jamie would have said *good* and that would have been that. The not-mentioning is information. The not-mentioning is me knowing that the purchase was a little bit about something other than boots and not wanting to put it down on the table where it would have to be looked at.

Here is the thing I keep coming back to, though, and the reason I am writing about boots instead of about Pat's email which I also have not answered. The boots will be here Thursday. I will put them on. I will walk the dog in them, probably for years. They will get scuffed and conditioned and re-soled and they will outlast this whole strange period of my life, and at some point in 2031 I will be lacing them up to do something I cannot currently picture and the fact that I bought them at a weird ten p.m. moment in the ninth month of an unfinished thing will be a small footnote nobody including me will remember. Objects don't know what mood you were in when you got them. They just become part of the furniture of the days.

Which is maybe also true of this whole stretch. I don't know. The coffee is cold. I should warm it up or pour it out, and instead I keep drinking it cold, which is, I notice, also a thing.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a recent stretch of time — an afternoon, an evening, a Saturday morning — that felt like *you* in a way the rest of the week didn't. What were you doing, who was around, and what was different about how you were inside it?
entries.content:
Saturday morning, two weekends ago, the one where it rained hard from about six until nine and then stopped and the light came in low and yellow under the cloud line the way it does in October. Jamie had taken Theo to the orthodontist, which is a forty-minute round trip plus the actual appointment, so the house was me and Nina and the dog and nothing on the calendar until lunch. Nina had been working on a thing for school — a project about, I want to say, an ecosystem? She had a posterboard and she was drawing a marsh on it with markers and she had taken over the dining room table without asking, which is something she does now, which I think is a development I am supposed to be glad about.

I made a second pot of coffee. I sat down across from her at the table not to supervise, because she does not need supervising and would resent it, but because the kitchen was where the coffee was and the dining room was where she was, and it seemed nicer to be in the same room. I had a book. I had been meaning to read this book for about a month — it had been sitting on the arm of the couch with the receipt still in it as a bookmark — and I opened it and started actually reading it, not the way I read at night where I get through a page and a half and realize I have been thinking about something else, but actually reading.

And we just sat there for, I want to say, an hour and forty minutes. Maybe longer. Nina drew her marsh. I read my book. The dog was on the rug between us doing the thing where she sighs every few minutes like she is being put-upon by the quality of her own life. Every so often Nina would say something — *what's another word for muddy* — and I would answer without looking up — *boggy, marshy, swampy, soggy* — and she would say *swampy, that's good* and I wouldn't say anything else and neither would she. The rain stopped and I noticed it had stopped because the sound of it stopping is its own sound. The yellow light came in. I got up once to refill my coffee and once to let the dog out and back in and then I sat back down.

What was different about it. I have been trying to put my finger on this for two weeks and I think the closest I can get is: I was not, in that hour and forty minutes, performing being anything. I was not performing being a working person, which I do for myself in the mornings at the desk. I was not performing being a mom, which I do without realizing I am doing it whenever I am in the orbit of either of the kids and there is something to manage. I was not performing being someone who has it together for some imaginary observer in a Subaru. I was a person reading a book at a table with her kid drawing a marsh.

And the strange part — the part I keep coming back to — is that this is what I think I thought I was buying when I left the job. Not boots, not a coaching practice, not a clear narrative for the rec center parking lot. This. A morning with no shape to it where I get to be in the same room as my eleven-year-old without either of us having anywhere to be, where the rain stops and I get to know that the rain stopped because nothing else is making noise. I had it for an hour and forty minutes and it was, I want to say without sounding ridiculous, the most clearly I have felt like myself in months.

It scares me to write that down because it is the answer to a question I am not supposed to be asking. The question I am supposed to be asking is *what is the work, what is the offer, what is the shape of it.* The Saturday morning is not an answer to that question. The Saturday morning is, if anything, an answer to a different question that I did not know I was also asking, which is something like *what is this for.* And the answer the Saturday morning gave is: *it is for being able to sit at a dining room table in October light and read a book while your kid draws a marsh.* Which is not a sentence I can put on a website. Which is not a sentence that closes any rec center windows. Which is also, possibly, true.

Theo came home with Jamie around eleven-thirty and the spell broke the way spells break, gently, with someone needing a snack and the dog losing her mind about Theo's return as if he had been gone for a year and not an hour. I put the book down with the receipt still in it. I have not picked it back up. I keep meaning to and then it is night and I am tired.

I did not tell Jamie about the morning. That is two things now I have not told Jamie about — the boots and the marsh morning — and I notice they are not the same kind of not-telling. The boots not-telling was the kind where you don't want the thing examined. The morning not-telling is the kind where you don't want the thing handled. If I described it to Jamie at dinner — *and then we just sat there for almost two hours and it was so nice* — Jamie would say something kind and accurate and the morning would become a small story we have, which is fine, that is what marriages do with mornings. I just wanted to keep it as the thing it was for a little while longer before it became a story.

Nina's marsh, by the way, got an A. She brought it home folded and put it on top of the recycling and I almost said something and then didn't.

entries.prompt: Is there someone you've been meaning to reach out to, and haven't? Describe the last time you almost did it — where you were, what stopped you, and what you ended up doing instead.
entries.content:
Pat. Obviously Pat. The email has been sitting in my inbox since the Tuesday before last, which makes it — I am counting on my fingers — eleven days. Eleven days is past the window where you can reply normally. Eleven days is into the territory where the reply itself has to acknowledge the not-replying, which makes the reply a bigger thing than the original email warranted, which makes you not reply, which makes it twelve days, thirteen.

The email is not even hard. Pat sent over a draft of something — a framework, Pat is calling it a framework, I am trying not to have feelings about the word framework — and asked if I would take a look and tell Pat what I think. Casual tone. *No rush, whenever you have a sec.* The kind of ask that should take an hour to read and forty minutes to respond to and instead has become a small weather system over the corner of my desk.

The last time I almost answered it was Thursday night. I had cleared the dishes, Jamie was upstairs with Nina doing something about a permission slip, Theo was in his room doing whatever Theo does in his room, and I sat down at the laptop with a fresh cup of decaf — a thing I have started doing, decaf at night, which feels like a small surrender I am not ready to write about — and I opened the email and I read it again, the third or fourth time. And I started typing. *Pat — finally getting to this, sorry for the lag —* and I got that far and I stopped, because *finally getting to this* implied that the next thing I was going to say was a substantive response to the framework, and I did not have a substantive response to the framework, because I had not actually read the framework. I had skimmed it. Twice. The way you skim something when you are looking to find out how you feel about it before you commit to reading it properly.

What stopped me, if I am being honest, is that I am afraid Pat's framework is good. I am afraid I will read it carefully and discover that it is the thing I have been failing to write for nine months, and that Pat has written it in what I can tell from the skim is about three weeks of working on it, and that the response I will then have to send will be either the generous one — *this is great, really clear, here are some small thoughts* — or the protective one — *interesting, I wonder about X and Y,* where X and Y are objections I am raising because I cannot bear to just say *this is great.* I do not want to find out which of those two people I am right now. So I closed the laptop. I told myself I would read it properly Friday morning at the desk. Friday morning I worked on the Google doc instead, the same top section I have been rewriting since August, and at eleven I went for a walk and at lunch I made a sandwich and the email sat there.

What I ended up doing Thursday night, after closing the laptop, was watching most of a baking show with Jamie, the kind where nothing bad happens and everyone is gentle with each other, which is a genre I did not used to have time for and now apparently need. Jamie fell asleep on the couch around the technical challenge. The dog moved from the rug to the spot Jamie's feet had been. I sat there and watched the rest of it with the volume low and thought about Pat for approximately none of it, which was the point, and also thought about Pat for all of it, underneath, which was also the point.

Here is the thing I want to write down because I think it is the actual thing. Pat and I started talking about this work — this thing, this whatever-it-is — around the same time, last winter, on a phone call that went long. We were both still in the old shapes of our lives then, both circling the same exit. Pat got out about two months after I did. And from the outside I would have said we were doing the same thing on roughly the same timeline, which is the kind of thing that lets you have someone to compare notes with without it being a competition. Except now it's eleven months for me and nine for Pat and Pat has a framework and I have a Google doc whose top section I have rewritten enough times that I have started to recognize my own old sentences as if they were furniture in a hotel room.

I am not sure I am angry about this. I am not sure *envy* is even the right word, although it is in the neighborhood. The closer word might be *exposed.* Pat sending the framework exposes the difference between what each of us has done with the same months. And the not-replying is me trying to keep the curtain over the exposure for as long as I can, which everybody including Pat can probably see through, which makes the curtain itself part of the exposure. There is no good move from here except to read the thing and write the response, and I know this, and I have known this for a week, and I have not done it.

What I am going to do, I think — what I am writing down so I see it tomorrow — is read it Monday morning before I do anything else. Not at the desk, where I will start editing my reply in my head before I have finished reading. At the kitchen table, on paper if I print it, with coffee, the way I would read a friend's draft if I were not also auditioning to be a person who has frameworks. And then I will write back. Not the generous version, not the protective version. Whatever I actually think. Pat asked. Pat is allowed to have asked.

The dog has just put her chin on my foot, which is her way of saying it's time for the walk. I am going to take her. The boots, by the way, are great.

entries.prompt: When was the last time you laughed — really laughed, the kind that surprises you? Tell me where you were, who you were with, and what set it off.
entries.content:
Friday night, in the car, coming back from the pizza place we go to when nobody wants to cook, which by Friday is usually nobody. All four of us. Theo in the front because his legs have gotten long enough that putting him in the back feels cruel, Nina in the back next to me because Jamie was driving, the pizza box on Nina's lap because that is the only flat surface that exists in our car, and the dog at home presumably committing crimes against the couch.

What set it off was Theo. Theo, who has been quiet for most of the fall in the way fourteen-year-old boys go quiet, like a radio someone turned the volume down on without changing the station. Theo, who at dinner mostly eats and answers questions with the smallest grammatically complete unit available. Theo did an impression of his social studies teacher. Out of nowhere. We had been talking about something else — I think Nina was complaining about a kid in her class — and Theo said, in this voice that was not his voice, *that's a great question, and I want to honor it, but I also want to redirect us,* and Jamie laughed first, the surprised laugh, because Jamie has met this teacher at conferences and apparently the impression was accurate enough that Jamie knew who it was before Theo said the name. And then Nina laughed because Jamie was laughing, and then Theo, encouraged, did another one — *let's park that and come back to it* — and then a third that involved a specific thing the teacher does with his hands, which Theo demonstrated in the front seat, and that was the one that got me.

I laughed so hard I made a sound I do not usually make. Some kind of honk. Nina lost it about the honk, which made Jamie lose it about Nina losing it about the honk, which made Theo, who had been pleased with himself in a contained way, break into the actual laugh, the open one, the one I have not heard from him in I do not know how long. Months, maybe. The pizza box slid halfway off Nina's lap and she caught it without stopping laughing, and Jamie had to slow the car down because Jamie's eyes were watering, and I was bent over with my hand on my chest like a person in a cartoon, and the four of us made it through a whole stoplight cycle just trying to get our breath back, and every time it almost stopped one of us would look at another one of us and it would start again.

I cannot tell you the last time the four of us laughed like that together. I want to say it has been a year. It might have been longer. It is the kind of thing you do not notice the absence of until it comes back, and then you realize the room has been quieter than you knew.

What I keep thinking about, sitting here Sunday morning with the coffee, is that Theo did the impression. Theo, who I have been worried about in the soft chronic way you worry about a kid who has gone underground for a developmental reason and may or may not come back up on schedule. Theo had been watching this teacher closely enough to do him. Theo had been carrying the bit around, waiting for the right car ride. Theo wanted to make us laugh. The wanting-to is the part that got me, more than the impression itself. Some part of him is still oriented toward us, is still looking for the moment, is still composing the material in his head while he sits in social studies. I had started to assume he wasn't. I had started to file him under *we'll see him again at twenty.* And here he was, fourteen and in the front seat, doing crowd work.

I do not know if I would have caught it a year ago. I think I would have laughed — it was a good impression, anyone would have laughed — but I am not sure I would have *caught* it the way I caught it Friday, which is the part that has stayed with me. I would have been thinking about Monday. I would have been half in an email in my head. The version of me that was in the back seat on Friday was all the way in the back seat, and so when Theo did the thing I felt the whole shape of it — the watching, the waiting, the offering — and not just the punchline. That is a thing this strange year has given me that I do not know how to put on a website either.

We got home and the laughing had mostly stopped but it kept catching us in small aftershocks. Nina said *honor it* in the wrong voice while she was getting out of the car and Jamie snorted. The dog lost her mind about the pizza. Theo carried the box in and went upstairs almost immediately, the way he does, but he was smiling on the stairs, and I saw him see me see him smiling, and he did not stop smiling, which is its own small thing.

Later, in bed, Jamie said *Theo was funny tonight,* and I said *Theo was so funny,* and Jamie said *I had no idea he had that,* and I said *me neither,* and we lay there for a minute not saying anything, and I think we were both doing the same small math about how much of our kid we have been seeing and how much we have not.

I told Jamie about the marsh morning, then. I had not meant to. It came out because we were already in the territory of things-about-the-kids-we-had-not-said. Jamie listened and said *that sounds nice,* and that was all, and it was the right amount, and the morning is now a small story we have, and I find I do not mind. Maybe two weeks was long enough to keep it as the thing it was. Maybe the reason to keep some things to yourself for a while is just to make sure they are yours before you let them be ours.

The coffee is still hot. The dog is asleep. Pat's framework, which I read Monday and replied to Tuesday, was good, by the way. Not the thing I have been failing to write. A different thing. I will write about that another day.

entries.prompt: Think of a time recently when you were the most patient person in the room, and a time you were the least. What was the difference between those two versions of you?
entries.content:
The patient version was Wednesday afternoon at Mom's kitchen table. I had driven down because she had asked me to look at something with her insurance — a letter she had gotten that she did not understand and that, when I read it, did not require understanding, because it was a notice that nothing was changing, written in the language of things changing. I explained this to her. She did not believe me. She handed me the letter again and pointed at a sentence in the middle and said *but what about this part,* and I read the part, which said the same thing the rest of the letter said, and I explained it again in different words. She nodded. She put the letter down. Forty seconds later she picked it back up and said *but what about this part.*

We did this, I want to say, six times. Maybe seven. I made us tea in the middle of it. I got up and looked at the bird feeder out the window because she wanted me to see that the cardinal was back. I sat back down and we did the letter again. At one point she asked if I thought she should call the company, and I said she could, and she said *but what would I say,* and I said *you could say what you said to me, that you wanted to make sure nothing was changing,* and she said *but I don't know if anything is changing,* and we were back at the beginning, and I did not feel anything tighten in my chest. I just started over.

I do not know where the patience came from. It is not always there with her. There are calls where I am unkind in my head before the second sentence, where every *well, I'm sure something will come together soon* lands like a thumb on a bruise and I get off the phone and walk it off in the yard. But Wednesday I had nowhere to be. That is part of it. I had driven down with no return time pinned to anything, and the afternoon was, in a way the old afternoons never were, actually available. The other part, which I am slower to admit, is that she looked small at the table. She has always been a person who took up a room, and at the table with the letter she looked like someone who needed the same sentence said to her until it stuck, and I had the sentence, and I had the time, and there was no reason in the world not to give them to her. So I did. I drove home in the dark and felt — not virtuous, the word is too clean — but settled. Like I had spent the afternoon on something the right size for the afternoon.

The impatient version was the next morning, with Nina, about her shoes.

She could not find her shoes. The specific shoes, the ones she wears every day, which live by the door, which had not moved, which were under the bench where they always are, which she had looked at and not seen because she was looking for them somewhere else. We had eight minutes until the bus. I was in the kitchen making her lunch one-handed while pouring coffee with the other and she came in and said *I can't find my shoes,* and I said, without looking up, *they're by the door,* and she said *I looked,* and I said *look again,* and she said *I LOOKED,* and something in me — I want to be precise about this — went hot and fast and small. I put the coffee down too hard. I said *Nina. They are where they always are. Go look. With your eyes.* In a voice I did not love. She went, found them in three seconds, came back, did not look at me, put them on, did not look at me, got her backpack, did not look at me, and left for the bus.

I stood in the kitchen with the lunch half-made and felt awful for about forty-five minutes.

What was the difference. I have been turning this over since Thursday and I think the cheap answer is *time,* which is true — I had all afternoon at Mom's and eight minutes with Nina — but it is not the whole answer, because I have had eight-minute mornings with Nina before and not gone hot in them. The truer answer, I think, is who I was being asked to be. With Mom at the table I was being asked to be a daughter who could read a letter and explain it, which is a role I know how to occupy and am not currently in any kind of crisis about. With Nina in the kitchen at 7:52 I was being asked to be a competent morning person, the dispatcher, the mother who has the lunch packed and the coffee poured and the kid out the door, and I am — I notice — slightly raw about competence right now. I am running low on the feeling of being good at things. And so when an eleven-year-old came in and presented me with a problem that should have been beneath the system, my response was to defend the system, which did not need defending from her, which was not even really about her.

Mom asking the same question seven times did not threaten anything in me. Nina not finding her shoes did, which is absurd on its face and exact when I look at it. The patience I had in the afternoon was the patience of a person with nothing to prove to the person in front of her. The impatience in the morning was the impatience of a person trying to prove something to herself with a kid as the unwilling audience.

I apologized to Nina after school. I said I had been short with her and I was sorry. She said *it's fine,* in the voice that means *I have already filed it,* and asked if she could have a snack, and that was that. Kids are forgiving in a way that is almost unfair, because it lets you off a hook you should maybe stay on a little longer.

I am going to try to stay on it a little longer. The coffee is cold again. I should go.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of moment where someone says something to you and your face does one thing while your insides do another. Has that happened recently? Tell me what they said, what your face did, and what was actually going on underneath.
entries.content:
Sunday at the grocery store, in the cereal aisle, of all places. I had run in for milk and eggs and the things you go in for and come out having spent sixty dollars on, and I turned the corner and there was Marcus, who used to be two rungs up from me at the old place, with a cart and a daughter I did not recognize because the last time I saw her she was in elementary school and now she was taller than her father and on her phone. We did the thing. *Oh my god, hi.* The hug that is not quite a hug because of the carts. The how-are-yous. He looked good. He looked like a person who had been sleeping. We caught up for about ninety seconds — his daughter wandered off toward the cereal she actually wanted — and then he said, in the warm voice he has always had, the voice that made him good at his job, *you know, I have to say, I really admire what you did. I think about it. I don't know if I could have done it.*

My face smiled. My face did the gracious thing, the slight head-tilt, the *oh, thank you, that's kind of you to say.* My face was, I think, convincing. Marcus would have walked away from that interaction thinking he had said a nice thing and it had landed nicely, which is, to be fair to my face, the correct outcome of the interaction. Nobody needs Marcus to walk to his car worried about me.

What was happening underneath was harder to name in the moment and I have been trying to name it since.

The first thing was a small flare of pleasure, which I want to be honest about because it is embarrassing. Marcus admires me. Marcus, who I used to slightly perform for in meetings, thinks about what I did. Some twenty-year-old part of me lit up like a porch light. *Someone from the old place sees me as the brave one.* That feeling was there for about a second and a half and then it curdled, because the second thing arrived right behind it, which was: Marcus is admiring a version of the story that is much cleaner than the version I am living. Marcus has constructed, in his head, a Riley who quit on purpose to do a thing, and the Riley he is admiring is the Riley of the answer I did not give the rec center mom. *I left a twenty-year career to do this on purpose.* He has already given me the benefit of the doubt I have been failing to give myself. He has, without knowing it, written my LinkedIn announcement for me.

And the third thing, which is the one that has stayed, is that I felt — I am going to use the word and then qualify it — *fraudulent.* Not because what I did was not brave-ish, in some accounting. It was. But because the bravery he was admiring had, in his telling, a shape and a direction, and the actual nine months has had neither, and the gap between his admiration and my situation was, standing there in front of the granolas, vertiginous. He thinks I jumped to something. I jumped off something. Those are not the same verb.

Underneath that was a fourth thing, which I am even slower to put down. I wanted to ask him how it was going. I wanted to ask him about the project he had been running when I left, about whether so-and-so was still there, about the reorg I had heard rumors of. I wanted to ask in a way that I recognized, with some discomfort, was not pure curiosity. I wanted to hear that it was hard. I wanted to hear that the place was worse without me, which is a sentence I am embarrassed to have typed but which is what was in there. I did not ask. I said *how are things at the old shop,* in the breezy voice, and he said *oh, you know, same circus,* and we both laughed the small laugh you laugh at that, and his daughter came back with a box of cereal he did not approve of, and we said the goodbye sentences, and he pushed his cart toward the dairy and I stood there for a second pretending to read the side of a granola box.

What my face did was kind. What my face did was protect both of us from the awkwardness of me telling Marcus, in the cereal aisle, that I am not as far along as he thinks I am, which would have been a thing to do to Marcus and not for him. The face was correct. I do not regret the face.

What I am sitting with is the fact that the inside took about forty minutes to settle down after, and that I drove home with the groceries thinking about Marcus's sentence — *I don't know if I could have done it* — and trying to figure out whether he was right to admire it or whether I was right to feel like a fraud, and I think the unsatisfying answer is that both can be true at the same time and there is no scoreboard that resolves it. He saw a person who did a thing he has thought about doing. I am a person who is, on a Sunday, in a grocery store, in the ninth month of doing it, with no clear story about what it has produced. Both pictures are accurate. Neither is the whole picture. The whole picture is not available to anyone, including me, which is part of what makes the cereal aisle hard.

What I notice, writing this down, is that I did not tell Jamie about Marcus when I got home. I unpacked the groceries and put the milk away and Jamie said *get everything,* and I said *yeah, mostly,* and that was the moment to say *ran into Marcus,* and I did not. Three things now. The boots, the marsh morning, Marcus. The marsh morning I told eventually. The boots I still have not. Marcus I will probably mention at dinner tonight, casually, *oh I ran into so-and-so,* and Jamie will say *oh how is he,* and I will say *good, same,* and the inside of the cereal aisle will not come up, because there is no graceful way to bring it up and because I am not sure, yet, what I would say about it.

The dog wants out. I am going.

entries.prompt: Last week you wrote about almost reaching out to Pat and not doing it. Where does that sit now — has the email moved, or have you moved around it?
entries.content:
The email has moved. I wrote about that already, in passing, at the end of the laughing-in-the-car entry — *Pat's framework, which I read Monday and replied to Tuesday, was good, by the way.* I tossed it off because I wanted to be the kind of person who could toss it off, and I think I wanted to reward myself for finally doing the thing by treating the doing as casual. It was not casual. I am going to write about it properly now because the prompt is asking and because I notice I have been carrying it around like a small pebble in a pocket, not heavy, just there.

I did what I said I was going to do. Monday morning, kitchen table, printed it out, coffee. I read the whole thing twice before I picked up a pen. The first read I tried to read it the way I would read a stranger's, which is impossible — Pat's voice is in the sentences, I could hear Pat saying them — but I tried. The second read I made marks. Small marks. A check next to the parts that landed, a question mark next to the parts I wanted to push on, a wavy line next to the parts I thought were doing more work in Pat's head than on the page. There were more checks than question marks. I will not pretend that didn't cost me something to admit to myself in real time.

It is not the thing I have been failing to write. That is the thing I want to put down clearly because Thursday night, when I was avoiding it, I had told myself it would be. The fear was that I would open the document and see my unwritten thing already written, in another voice, finished. What I actually saw was Pat's thing — Pat's actual thing, which is shaped by Pat's actual life and Pat's actual mind, with concerns I do not share and emphases I would not have chosen and a vocabulary I find slightly too clean. It is good. It is genuinely good. It is also unmistakably Pat's, which means it is not mine, which means the thing I have been failing to write is still mine to either write or not write. This was a relief and also, somehow, harder, because it took away the excuse of *Pat got there first* and left only *I have not gotten there yet.* The not-having-gotten-there is on me. It is not a race I lost. It is a thing I have not done.

I wrote the response Tuesday morning. It took about forty minutes, which is roughly what it should have taken, which is humbling given that I held the email for eleven days. I did not do the generous version and I did not do the protective version. I told Pat what I thought. I said the framework was clear in the places where I had been unable to be clear in my own work, and I told Pat which two sections I thought were carrying the weight. I asked one real question about the third section, where I thought the logic was thinner than the prose was making it sound — I phrased it as a question because it is a question, not because I was hiding behind one. I did not reference the eleven days. I started the email *Pat —* and then went straight in. *Read this carefully over coffee. Here's what I think.* That was it. No apology preamble. The apology preamble would have made the email about me.

Pat wrote back within the hour. *This is exactly what I needed, thank you, the third section is the one I have been fighting with, you named it.* That was a good email to receive. I sat with it for a minute before I closed it. Then I closed it.

Where it sits now. The thing I did not expect is that having answered Pat has not made me feel further behind. I had assumed, in the eleven days of not-answering, that engaging with Pat's framework would sharpen the contrast — Pat's done thing next to my undone thing, like two photographs on a wall. What actually happened is that reading something Pat made carefully, and responding to it carefully, reminded me that I know how to do this. Not how to write the framework — that I still do not know — but how to read a thing, see what it is doing, see what it is not doing, say so. That is a competence I have. It did not go anywhere when I left the job. It was sitting in me the whole time and I had not used it on anything that mattered for months, because rewriting the top of my own Google doc does not require it. You cannot read your own thing the way you can read someone else's. I had forgotten what it felt like to use the part of my brain that works on someone else's draft. It felt good. It felt like a tool I had been missing without knowing I was missing it.

Which is making me wonder, in a way I am not ready to commit to on the page, whether the thing I am supposed to be building has more to do with that — reading other people's drafts of the lives they are trying to make — than with the framework I keep failing to write about it. The Google doc keeps trying to be a manifesto. The forty minutes with Pat's framework was not a manifesto. It was just work, on someone else's thing, that I happened to be good at. I do not want to overread this. I have overread smaller things this fall and embarrassed myself privately. But I am going to let myself notice it.

The other thing that has moved is that Pat and I have a coffee on the calendar for next week. Pat suggested it in the reply email — *want to talk through the third section sometime?* — and I said yes before I could rehearse. I have not started rehearsing yet. I am sure I will. But I said yes quickly, which is, for me, a small data point.

The pebble is still in the pocket. It is just smaller now, and a different shape, and I can put my hand around it without flinching.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel relief that something was over — a conversation, a task, an evening, a stretch of days? Describe the moment it ended and what loosened in you.
entries.content:
Friday, around four-fifteen, when I shut the laptop after the call with the dentist's office about Theo's appointment and realized that the call had been the last thing on the list, and that the list itself — which had built up over the week in that way lists do, where each item is small but the aggregate becomes a kind of low hum — was done. Not done well. Just done. The dentist, the two emails about Nina's after-school thing, the form for Mom's pharmacy that had to be printed and signed and scanned and sent back, the call with the guy about the gutters, the reschedule with the woman I had been supposed to talk to Tuesday and then Wednesday and then finally Friday. The call with the woman, by the way, was fine. They are mostly fine. It is the anticipation that costs.

What loosened. I want to be honest about what loosened, because it was not what I expected.

I had thought, all week, that what was making me tight was the work-shaped items — the call with the woman, mostly, which I had rehearsed for in the bad way, the way where you write down sentences you are going to say casually. But when I shut the laptop, the loosening was not in the chest, where the work-shaped tightness usually sits. It was lower and broader, in the way you feel relief when a houseguest leaves. Not a houseguest you disliked. Just a houseguest, whose presence had required a layer of attention you had not noticed putting on until it came off.

The list had been the houseguest. The week had been the list.

I sat at the kitchen table for a minute without doing anything. The dog noticed I had stopped and came over. Outside the light was already going, which it does now at four-fifteen, which I am still adjusting to. I did not get up to start dinner. I did not open another tab. I did not pick up the phone. I sat there and let the quiet be quiet, and I felt the specific sensation of nothing being scheduled and nothing being owed, and I thought — and this is the part that has stayed with me — *oh, this is what Saturday morning was.* Not the marsh, not the book, not the light. This. The absence of the thing pressing.

Which made me realize, sitting there, that the marsh morning had not been a special morning. It had been a normal morning that I had had access to because nothing was pressing. The pressing has been the thing all along. I have been telling myself, since I left, that I have so much more time than I used to, and the math is true, the calendar is mostly empty, but the *pressing* has not left. I have just been doing it to myself. The list on Friday was a list I had made. Nobody had handed it to me. The woman on Wednesday I had moved to Friday because I wanted to move her, not because I had to. I had been making the houseguest and then exhausting myself by hosting.

I do not want to make this into a lesson. I notice I am about to and I am going to try not to. The lesson version would be *therefore stop making lists,* which is not true and not useful, because some of the items on the list were real and had to be done by someone and that someone, in the current arrangement, is me. The Mom pharmacy form had to happen. The dentist had to be rescheduled. What loosened on Friday at four-fifteen was not the recognition that the list had been unnecessary. It was the recognition that the list had been finite, and had ended, and that the ending was a real ending and not just a pause before the next list.

I am going to try to say this carefully. In the old job, the list never ended. That was the deal. You closed one and another one was already arriving, and the closing was not a moment, it was a transition between two states of having things to do. I had forgotten what it felt like for the list to actually be empty for an evening. Friday at four-fifteen the list was empty. I did not have anything I was supposed to be doing for the rest of the day. Jamie was not home yet. The kids were not home yet. The dog was looked after. There was no email I was avoiding, which is a sentence I could not have written six weeks ago when Pat's was sitting there. There was nothing.

What I did with the nothing was small. I made a fresh pot of coffee even though it was four-fifteen and I would regret it at eleven, which I did. I stood at the window for a while watching the light go. The neighbor across the street was raking, which seems pointless this time of year because more leaves are coming, but he does it anyway, and I watched him do it for longer than was reasonable. I thought about calling Mom and decided not to, which is also a kind of relief, the kind where you give yourself permission not to do the optional good thing. I thought about Pat's coffee next week and noticed I was not yet rehearsing for it, and gave myself a small private credit for that, and then immediately worried that giving myself credit was its own kind of rehearsal, and then laughed at myself a little, in the kitchen, alone.

The kids came home in a wave around five. Theo with a friend, briefly, which is new. Nina with a story about the bus. Jamie around six with a bag of something for dinner because Jamie had also decided not to cook, which we had not coordinated and which made us both laugh when we figured it out. The evening became an evening. The loosening from four-fifteen did not last, exactly, but it had been there, and I knew I had had it, and that was different from the weeks where I do not notice the loosening because I refill the list before the loosening can land.

What I want to write down before I lose it: the relief on Friday was not relief that the work was over. It was relief that I had let the work be over. Those are not the same. The first is something that happens to you. The second is something you do. I am not sure I have been doing it, most weeks. I am going to try to do it more, and I am also going to try not to make *doing it more* into a new item on the list, which is exactly the kind of thing I would do.

The coffee, by the way, was a mistake. I was up until almost one. Worth it.

entries.prompt: Where in your week did you find yourself performing competence you didn't actually feel? Pick the moment and tell me what was going on around you, and what you would have said if you'd had the nerve.
entries.content:
Tuesday, on the call with the guy I have been calling, in my head, the maybe-client. Not maybe-client to his face. To his face he is just Daniel, who got my name from someone who got it from someone, and who wanted to talk about whether what I do might be useful to him. Forty-five minutes scheduled. I had cleared the morning. I had made notes. I had, God help me, made notes about the notes — a little outline of how the conversation might go, what I might ask in the first ten minutes, what I might say if he asked about pricing, which I had decided I would handle by not handling it, by saying *let's see if there's a fit first.* I had read that sentence in something somewhere and I had filed it and now I was going to use it.

He asked about pricing in the first eight minutes.

I said the sentence. I said *let's see if there's a fit first,* in a voice that I think sounded warm and unhurried, the voice of a person who has said this many times to many people. I had not. I had said it once, into a kitchen, to no one, while practicing. He said *sure, that makes sense,* and we kept going. And the rest of the call I was, I want to say, two people. One was conducting the call. The other was watching the first one conduct the call and grading her in real time. *Good question. That landed. Don't say "totally" again. Why did you just say "totally" again.* Daniel was talking about his situation and I was nodding into the camera and making the small noises and asking the follow-up questions, and underneath I was running a parallel process where I was performing for myself the role of a person who does this for a living.

Which I do, technically. I am, technically, doing this for a living, in the sense that I am not doing anything else and this is what I have said the thing is. But I do not, yet, do it the way the person on the call was pretending to do it. The person on the call had a process. The person on the call had a *we* — *here is how we typically start, here is what we usually find* — when in fact the *we* is one woman at a kitchen desk in a house with a dog. The plural was a costume. I had put it on for the call without deciding to. It came out of my mouth the second time as if it had always been there.

What was going on around me, physically, was that the dog was on the rug behind me doing the breathing, and the radiator had started its afternoon clanking, and the light in the room was the four-thirty light that I am trying to make peace with. I had closed the door so the radiator and the dog would not be on the call. I had a cup of water. I had positioned the laptop so the bookshelf was behind me, because someone told me once that bookshelves read as competent. I had thought about that. I had positioned it. I am writing this down because I want to be accurate about how much staging there was. There was a lot of staging. The staging was for an audience of one man on a screen who was, I am pretty sure, doing his own version of the same thing on his end.

What I would have said if I had the nerve. I have been chewing on this since Tuesday afternoon and I do not think the nervy version is *I am new at this and figuring it out as I go,* which is the answer that sounds brave when you imagine giving it and would actually be a small disaster on a first call with someone deciding whether to hire you. Daniel does not need my interior weather. Daniel needs to know whether I can help him think about his thing. The nervy version, the actually-nervy version, is something more like — I am working it out as I write — *I have done a version of this kind of thinking with people for a long time, in a different costume. I am still figuring out what the costume looks like in this new context. The thinking is the same. The thinking is good.* Said calmly. Without apology. Without the *we.*

That would have been more honest and also, I think, more useful, because the *we* was making me sound like a smaller version of a bigger firm and what Daniel actually seemed to want, when I listen back to the call in my head, was a person. A specific person, with specific judgment, who would tell him specific things. Every time I retreated into the *we* I was retreating from the thing he was reaching for. The competence I was performing was the competence of an institution, and he had not called an institution. He had called me.

I noticed this about thirty minutes in and could not figure out how to climb back out of the costume mid-call without it being weird. So I stayed in it. We ended pleasantly. He said he wanted to think about it and would be in touch next week. I said *of course, take the time you need,* in the costume voice, and we hung up, and I sat there for a minute with the camera off and felt the specific deflated feeling of having gotten through something by being a slightly smaller version of myself than the situation called for.

The thing I want to write down, because I think it is the actual thing: the costume is not protecting me from being seen as less competent. The costume is what is making me seem less competent, because the costume is generic and I am not. The Karen-or-not-Karen answer in the parking lot was the same problem in a different key — going generic when the specific would have served better. I keep choosing generic in the moments where I am most afraid, and I keep being surprised that generic does not land. Generic never lands. Generic is the sound of a person trying not to be wrong.

If Daniel comes back next week, I am going to try to take the costume off in the first five minutes. I do not know how yet. I am going to think about it. I am also going to try not to rehearse it into another costume, which is, I notice, what I am already starting to do.

The radiator is clanking again. I am going to walk the dog.

entries.prompt: Think about a compliment or piece of praise you've gotten recently — from anyone, about anything. Walk me through the moment you received it, and what you did with it in the hours after.
entries.content:
Jamie said something Sunday night that I have been carrying around since, and I want to write it down before I either inflate it or wave it off, both of which I am capable of.

We were in the kitchen after dinner. Theo had cleared his plate without being asked, which is a thing that has started happening once a week or so, like a comet. Nina was upstairs. The dishwasher was running. Jamie was wiping down the counter, which Jamie does in long deliberate sweeps that I find slightly mesmerizing and slightly maddening because I would have been done by now. I was at the table sorting through the mail pile, which is the kind of low-grade task I do at night when I cannot face the bigger thing. Jamie said, not looking up, *you're easier to be around lately. I don't know if you've noticed.*

That was the whole sentence. *You're easier to be around lately. I don't know if you've noticed.* Then Jamie kept wiping the counter.

What my face did was the small smile, the *oh yeah?* face, deflective. What came out of my mouth was *huh, well, low bar,* in the joke voice, which is the voice I use when I am trying to get a compliment out of the room before I have to handle it. Jamie said *I'm serious,* still wiping, and I said *I know, thank you,* in a more honest register, and Jamie said *okay,* and that was the end of the conversation. About fifteen seconds, total. Then we moved on to whether we had bread for lunches.

I did not sleep well Sunday. Not because of the sentence — the spreadsheet visit at eleven took care of that, the usual one, no new information, just the usual scrolling — but the sentence was in the room while I was lying there. *Easier to be around.* I kept turning it over. Easier than what. Easier than when. Was I hard to be around. For how long had I been hard to be around. Was Jamie telling me I had been hard to be around for the entire previous era and was now graciously letting me know the era was over. Was Jamie telling me Jamie had been waiting it out.

That was Sunday night. By Monday morning I had moved into a different mode with it, which was wanting to ask Jamie to elaborate. *Easier how. In what way. Since when.* I did not ask. I knew, even as I was constructing the questions in my head over the coffee, that asking would be a way of trying to convert the sentence into a longer sentence I could grade myself against, and Jamie had not given me the longer sentence on purpose. Jamie had given me the short one and gone back to the counter. The shortness was, I think, the point. A longer sentence would have been a report. The short one was a noticing.

By Monday afternoon I was somewhere else with it again. I had been at the desk doing the Google doc thing, the same top section, and I caught myself thinking — and this is the part I want to put down because it surprised me — *Jamie thinks I am easier to be around even though the doc is not done.* Even though the framework is not written. Even though there is no roster. Even though the cereal aisle and the parking lot. The easier-to-be-around is independent of the work-shaped progress I keep grading myself on. Jamie, who lives with me, who has watched the nine months from inside the house, who has done more of the dishes, who said *you have permission* and meant it and is also tired — Jamie is reporting on a different axis than the one I am scoring myself on. The axis Jamie is reporting on is the axis of what it is like to be in a room with me.

I do not know what to do with that. I want to be careful here because I can feel myself wanting to either make it small — *well, Jamie was being kind* — or make it big — *the whole thing is worth it because my spouse can breathe again.* Both of those are ways of not letting the sentence be the size it actually is. The size it actually is, I think, is: a person who has known me for a long time and watches me at close range told me, unprompted, on a Sunday night while wiping a counter, that the version of me who is currently in the house is more bearable than the version who used to be. That is information. It is not the whole picture and it is not nothing.

What I keep getting stuck on is the *I don't know if you've noticed* part. Because I have not noticed. I have been so busy noticing the things I am failing at that I have not noticed I am not, apparently, snapping at the people I live with the way I used to. I have not noticed that the dinner table is calmer. I have not noticed that I am the one who has stopped doing whatever it was I was doing — coming home charged, probably, full of something that needed to be discharged into the room before I could be in the room. I had assumed the calmer evenings were a kid thing, a Theo-grew-up thing, a Nina-finally-eats-vegetables thing. Maybe some of them are. But Jamie was telling me, gently, that some of them are me. That I have changed in a way I am not tracking, in the direction of being more pleasant to live with, while I have been busy worrying that I am turning into a person who buys boots she cannot quite explain and goes generic in parking lots.

I told Jamie at breakfast Tuesday that I had been thinking about what was said Sunday night and that I appreciated it. Jamie said *good,* and poured more coffee, and that was that. Which was the right amount, again. I am noticing Jamie is good at the right amount. I am noticing this is also a thing I have been able to see only because I am, apparently, easier to be around.

The dog needs out. The list today is short. I am going to try to let it stay short.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you've been putting off that isn't urgent but keeps surfacing in your head. When did you last almost do it, and what did you do instead?
entries.content:
The thing I keep almost doing and not doing is calling Chris. Not Pat — Pat is on the calendar, the coffee is Thursday, that one has resolved into something I am actually carrying out. Chris is different. Chris is the one I have not called since, I want to say, August. Maybe late July. There was a text exchange in September that ended with *we should really catch up properly* on both sides, in the way those exchanges end, and then neither of us did anything about it, and now it has been long enough that the calling has acquired the same problem the email to Pat had, which is that the call itself would now have to acknowledge the not-calling, which makes the call a bigger thing than it should be.

I almost did it Wednesday, on the walk. I was on the long loop, the one that goes past the school and around the pond, and the dog was being good, and I had my phone in my hand the way I do when I am thinking about calling someone, which is the tell. I scrolled to the name. I looked at the name. I thought about what time it was where Chris was — same time zone, no excuse there — and I thought about whether Chris would be at the desk or on a call or out, and I thought about whether I wanted to be the person who calls without warning, which Chris and I used to be with each other, and I am not sure we still are. The not-sureness was what stopped me. I put the phone back in the pocket of the coat and walked the rest of the loop and when I got home I made coffee and did not call.

What I did instead was, I noticed, almost worse. I went to the laptop and looked at Chris's LinkedIn. Not for any reason. Just to see. The profile had been updated — a small update, a new line under the current role about leading some initiative — and I read the line three times trying to figure out whether it represented a promotion or a sideways thing or a making-the-best-of-something, which is a kind of close reading I used to be fluent in and have not done in a while. I closed the tab feeling slightly grubby. I had gone to look at a friend the way you look at a stranger, and the looking had not given me any information I could use, and it had cost me something I cannot quite name. A small alignment. Some small thing about the friendship that requires you not to do that.

The reason I am not calling, if I sit with it, is not that I do not want to talk to Chris. I do. The reason is that the last time we talked, in July or whenever it was, I was still in the early part of this thing, still in the *figuring it out, lots of conversations, exciting and terrifying* mode, and I had a lot to say about it, and Chris had been a good listener, the kind who asks the question that opens the next paragraph instead of closing it. And now, four months later, I do not have new news. I have the same news, slightly more weathered. The Google doc is still the Google doc. The framework I have not written is still the framework I have not written. Daniel from the call has not come back yet. Pat has a thing and I do not. If I call Chris and Chris says *so how is it going,* I will have to answer, and the honest answer is *roughly the same as in July, but with more accumulated worry,* which is not a sentence you offer a friend on a Wednesday afternoon catch-up call. So I do not call. So the not-calling becomes its own data point about how it is going, which Chris, being smart, will eventually clock.

There is also — I am going to try to put this down accurately — a thing about Chris specifically that is making it harder. Chris is from the old life. Chris is one of the few from the old life I still have. And I think I have been, without quite admitting it, treating the friendship like it has a shelf life. Like the longer I am out, the less Chris and I have to talk about, because the shared substrate — the people, the rhythms, the inside jokes about the building — is getting fainter on my end. I have not been in the building in nine months. Chris has been in the building every week. The substrate is doing what substrates do. And rather than call and find out what we still have when the substrate is thinner, I have been not calling, and letting the thinning happen passively, which is, I notice, a way of confirming the fear without testing it.

What I almost did, the second almost, was draft a long email instead of calling. I got as far as the subject line — *thinking of you, overdue catch-up* — and I closed the laptop because I knew that what I was actually doing was looking for a way to do the connecting without the realtime of it, the part where Chris's voice would be on the other end and I would have to be a person in time with another person. The email would have been a performance of staying in touch. The call would be staying in touch. I know the difference. I have known the difference all week.

The thing I am going to try to do — not write down as a list item, just try — is call on Friday. Not rehearse it. Not make notes. Just call, in the late morning when Chris is usually between things, and if Chris does not pick up I will leave a short voicemail and that will be the move, and if Chris picks up I will say *hi, it has been too long, tell me about your fall,* and let Chris go first, which is, I realize as I write it, the move I should have made in July too. I kept making those calls about me. Chris let me. That is what good friends do, and it is also, possibly, part of why the calls got harder to initiate — the unconscious accounting of who is owed the floor.

The dog has the leash in her mouth, which is a new thing she is doing, and which is unambiguous. I am going.

entries.prompt: What's something you've been telling yourself about yourself lately — a story or label, good or bad — that you've started to wonder about? Where did you last catch it running?
entries.content:
The story is *I am not a finisher.* That is the one. It has been running in the background of most weeks for a while now, and I caught it, this time, on Tuesday morning at the desk, when I opened the Google doc for what must be the hundredth time and watched myself, with something like clinical interest, delete the second paragraph and start it again. I have rewritten the second paragraph of that doc — I am not exaggerating, I went and counted in the version history — eleven times since August. Eleven. Each time I have told myself I was sharpening it. What I was actually doing was avoiding paragraph three.

And the voice that supplies the meaning for this behavior, the voice that has been editorializing over my shoulder for months, says, in its calm parental way: *see, this is the pattern. You don't finish things. You start, you circle, you refine, and then you find a reason to begin again. The job you left, you didn't finish either — you walked off the field. The book you have been meaning to read since the marsh morning is still on the arm of the couch with the receipt in it. The framework you keep saying you are going to write. The thank-you notes from your birthday in May. You are not, fundamentally, a person who closes things.*

That is the story. Stated plainly it sounds almost silly. But it has been running, and I have been believing it, and the way I caught it Tuesday was that after I deleted paragraph two for the eleventh time I sat back and thought *of course, I'm a starter, not a finisher,* in exactly that resigned tone, and something about the resignation made me look at the sentence sideways. Because — wait. I finished a twenty-year career. You don't accidentally do twenty years. I finished raising — am finishing, am in the middle of — two kids who are reasonably intact human beings, one of whom did crowd work in a car last month. I finished the response to Pat's framework, which I had built up in my head as a thing I was incapable of doing, and which took forty minutes when I finally sat down. I have, in fact, finished a great many things. The story does not survive a serious audit.

So why is it running. I have been turning this over for two days and I think the answer is that the story is doing a job, and the job is protecting me from a different and harder story, which is something more like *I am a person who has not yet figured out what to finish.* That sentence is more accurate and much worse to sit with, because *not a finisher* is a stable identity I can settle into and grieve, whereas *has not yet figured out what to finish* is an open question that requires me to keep showing up to the desk. The first one closes. The second one stays open. I have been preferring the closed version, which is — I notice — itself a kind of finishing. I have been finishing the question prematurely by deciding the answer is that I am the kind of person who cannot.

The other place I caught it running was Sunday, watching the neighbor rake. I had this thought, watching him: *he will rake again next week and the week after and there is no version where the leaves are done, and he keeps raking anyway.* And the thought I had right after that, which I did not like, was *I could not do that. I would give up on the raking by mid-November.* Which is, first of all, not true — I rake — and second, was such a strange piece of self-attribution to layer onto a man I do not know doing yard work across a street. I was using him as a screen. The story was so loud it was casting itself onto strangers.

Where else. The boots — there is a version of the boots story where I bought them because *these are the boots of a person who is doing fine,* which I wrote down at the time, and there is another version, which I did not write down, where I bought them because they are a finished object. They arrived complete. They did not require me to keep iterating on them. The transaction had a beginning and an end. The doc does not. The framework does not. Maybe I bought a small finishable thing because I have been starving for the feeling of something being done.

The Saturday morning with Nina and the marsh — I told myself that morning was special because there was no pressing, and that is true, but I think it was also special because the morning had a shape. It started, it happened, it ended when Theo and Jamie came home. I read a chapter and a half. Nina finished her marsh. The marsh got an A. Things concluded. I have been starved for things concluding, and I have been telling myself the starvation is evidence of a character flaw — I cannot finish — when it might be evidence of something simpler, which is that the work I have currently set myself does not have natural endings yet, and a person without natural endings starts to feel like a person who cannot make them.

This is the part where I want to write a tidy resolution and I am going to try not to. I do not know yet whether the *not a finisher* story is going to loosen its grip just because I have caught it running. Stories about yourself are sticky. I will probably catch it again Thursday at the desk and believe it for an hour before I remember Tuesday. What I want to write down so I see it tomorrow is just this: the next time I hear that voice supplying the resigned conclusion, I am going to try to ask it what it is protecting me from having to do. I think I know the answer. I think the answer is paragraph three.

The coffee is cold. I am going to warm it up this time.
