entries.prompt: When did money — spending it, not spending it, looking at the number — last give you a feeling that wasn't really about money? Tell me where you were and what was going through your head.
entries.content:
Wednesday night, eleven-fifteen, the spreadsheet open on the laptop on the kitchen table, Jamie asleep upstairs, the dog at my feet doing the breathing. I had not gone to look at the spreadsheet for any reason. There was no new charge to reconcile, no bill that had come in, no decision pending. I had been reading on the couch, gotten up to put the book down, walked past the laptop, opened it, and the spreadsheet was already the front tab from the last time. So I looked. The way you look at a bruise to see if it is still there.

The number is the number. It has been the number, plus or minus the small motions of a household, for weeks. I know it the way I know my phone number. I can tell you what it was a month ago and what it was three months ago and what the slope between those two points looks like, because I have drawn that slope in my head probably a hundred times. The slope is not steep. The slope is a slope. We are still inside the *if we are careful this works for two years* sentence Jamie and I said to each other in February, and we are, by my count, about a third of the way through it. The math is fine. The math has been fine. The math is not what I was looking at.

What I was looking at, I realized after about four minutes of staring, was a small red square in the corner of one of the cells, which is how the spreadsheet flags a comment. I had left myself a comment in August and forgotten about it. I clicked on it. It said, in my own handwriting in the digital sense — my own typing — *check this against Q1 if revenue starts.* That was the comment. *If revenue starts.* I had typed that in August. It is now November.

And what came up in me, sitting there at eleven-fifteen with the dog breathing and the radiator clanking and the laptop light on my face, was not financial. The number had not moved. The slope had not changed. What I was feeling was the small private humiliation of having left myself a note in August that assumed a thing would have happened by now that has not happened, and finding the note like a piece of mail from a more optimistic version of myself. The August me thought there would be revenue to check against. The November me was the person being checked on, and was failing the check.

I want to be careful here because I can feel the spreadsheet thing wanting to become a bigger thing than it was. It was a comment in a cell. It took ten seconds to read. But I sat with it for, I want to say, twenty minutes, and what I was sitting with was not the question of whether we have enough money — we do, for now, by the careful definition — but the question of whether the August version of me would be disappointed in the November version, and whether the August version had a right to be, given that the August version did not know what the September and October and November versions were going to learn, which is that the work does not move on the timeline you write into a cell when you are feeling hopeful in the summer.

I deleted the comment. I want to say I deleted it cleanly but the truth is I right-clicked and hovered over *delete comment* for a beat before I did it, because deleting it felt like conceding something to someone, and I could not figure out who. The August me, probably. *No, we are not going to check this against Q1, because there is no Q1 to check against, and pretending there might be is a small dishonesty I am not going to keep maintaining.* Click. Gone. The cell looked the same. Nobody would ever know the comment had been there. I knew.

What was going through my head, after, was not about money. It was about the gap between the timeline I have been carrying around in some semi-private compartment and the timeline that is actually unfolding. I had not realized, until the comment, how much of the careful spreadsheet-checking has been me looking for evidence that the semi-private timeline is still on track, and finding, each time, that the numbers are the numbers and they do not speak to the timeline at all. The numbers say *you have this much, you are spending this much, here is how long that lasts at this rate.* The numbers do not say *and the work is going to come together in time.* I have been asking the numbers a question they cannot answer, and getting frustrated with them for not answering it, and calling the frustration *financial dread* when it is something else.

The something else, I think, is that I made myself a promise in February that I have not been keeping track of out loud. The promise was something like *give it eighteen months and see.* Two years was the financial runway. Eighteen months was the internal one. We are at nine. I am, by that internal clock, halfway. And the comment in the cell was the August version of me checking in on the halfway point and finding it bare, and I did not like being found.

I closed the laptop. I went upstairs. I did not wake Jamie. I lay there for a while doing the thing where you list to yourself all the reasons the math is fine, which is a comfort that works on the layer it is aimed at and not on the layer underneath. The layer underneath wanted me to admit that I have been using the spreadsheet as a stand-in for a conversation I have not had with myself about what the next nine months actually need to produce, if they are going to produce anything. The spreadsheet cannot have that conversation. The spreadsheet can only tell me whether we can afford to keep having it.

I fell asleep eventually. In the morning I made coffee and did not open the laptop until after the kids were gone, which is, I notice, slightly later than usual. A small avoidance. I am going to sit with the avoidance instead of fixing it. The number is the number. The question is mine.

entries.prompt: Who in your life have you been a little shorter with than they deserved lately? Take me into the moment it came out — what they said, what you said back, and what you were actually reacting to.
entries.content:
Mom, on Tuesday, on the phone, around six-thirty, while I was making dinner. The phone was on speaker on the counter because my hands were wet from rinsing rice, and the dog was underfoot because she had decided rice-rinsing was a thing requiring her supervision, and Nina was at the table doing math homework and pretending not to listen to the call, which she was listening to. Mom called outside the Wednesday window, which I noted at the time and noted again afterward. She does not usually call on Tuesdays. Tuesday is not the call.

She had called, ostensibly, about the pharmacy thing. The form I had printed and signed and scanned and sent in October, the one I wrote about — there had been a follow-up letter, and she wanted to know if she needed to do anything about it, and I said no, the follow-up was just a confirmation, and she said *are you sure,* and I said *yes, I am sure, it says right at the top "no action required,"* and she said *okay, well, I just wanted to check.* And then there was the small pause that means the call is not over, and then she said, in the voice that is supposed to sound casual and does not, *and how is the work going, are you finding things are picking up at all.*

What I said back was *Mom, it's fine, can we not do this tonight, I'm in the middle of dinner.*

I did not yell it. I did not say it cruelly. I said it flatly, in the voice of a person who has been asked the same question seven times and is on the seventh, even though she had only asked it once on this particular call. The flatness was the thing. The flatness was worse than volume would have been, because volume she could have pushed back on and flatness she could only absorb. There was a beat. She said *oh — okay, honey, I didn't mean to —* and I said *I know, I know, I'm sorry, I'm just — I'm making dinner, can I call you back this weekend,* and she said *of course, of course, you go,* in the small voice, the one that is trying to take up less room, and we hung up, and the rice was overcooked because I had stopped paying attention to it, and Nina did not look up from her homework but I could feel her not looking up, which is a specific quality of not-looking-up that eleven-year-olds have perfected.

I want to be accurate about what I was reacting to, because it was not the question. The question was the question she always asks. *Are things picking up.* It is the same question as *I'm sure something will come together soon,* in a slightly different costume. I have absorbed that question, in various costumes, probably forty times since February. I have not snapped at her about it before. So why Tuesday.

Tuesday was the day after I had reread the August comment in the spreadsheet. Tuesday was — I am putting these together as I write — the day I had spent the morning at the desk doing nothing useful and the afternoon walking the dog twice because I could not stand being at the desk. Tuesday I was already underwater on the question of whether things were picking up, because I had been asking myself the same question all day in the August voice, and Mom called and asked it in her voice, and the voices touched, and what came out was the flatness.

She was not the person I was short with. She was the person who walked into the room I was already short in.

Which is, I think, the thing about who you snap at. It is rarely about them. It is about who arrives during the weather. Mom arrived during the weather. The weather had been building since Wednesday night. She walked in at six-thirty on Tuesday and got it.

What makes it harder to put down is that the question she was asking is the question I most want to be asked by someone who will be satisfied by my answer, and she is the person least likely to be satisfied by any answer I give. Not because she is hard to please. Because she is worried, in the specific way mothers are worried about adult children whose lives have stopped looking like lives the mother can describe to her own friends. *Riley left her job and is starting something new* was a sentence Mom could say in February. *Riley is still figuring it out* is a sentence Mom cannot say comfortably in November, because in Mom's vocabulary *figuring it out* by month nine means something has gone wrong. So she calls and asks the question in the costume, and what she is really asking is *please tell me a sentence I can say to my friends,* and I do not have one yet, and instead of saying *I do not have one yet, Mom, and I am sorry, and I am working on it,* I said *can we not do this tonight,* which translated, in the kitchen, to *stop asking,* which is not what I meant and is what she heard.

I have not called back yet. It is Thursday. I told her the weekend, and the weekend is still the weekend, and I am going to call. I am going to call and not lead with the apology, because the apology will make the call about the snap and not about her, and what I want the call to be about is her week, her bird feeder, the cardinal, the neighbor she has been arguing with about the fence. I want to give her thirty minutes of being asked questions about her life by her daughter, which is, I am realizing, something I have not done in a while. I have been letting her drive every call because it is easier to bat away her questions about me than it is to ask my own about her. Batting is less work than asking. Asking requires presence. I have not been bringing presence to those calls. I have been bringing a script of deflections.

The call I should have made — the call the patient version of me at her kitchen table in October would have made — is the call where I say *Mom, tell me about your week,* and let her talk, and answer her question about my work in one short honest sentence at the end, and get off the phone before the sentence has to do more work than it can do. I know how to make that call. I made a version of it across her kitchen table about a letter. I can make it on the phone about a week.

The dog is at the door. The rice from Tuesday is still in the fridge. I will call her Saturday morning. From the porch, with coffee, before the day starts asking things of me.

entries.prompt: Where did you sleep badly this week, or wake up earlier than you wanted? Take me into the moment you gave up on going back to sleep — what was already running through your head before your feet hit the floor?
entries.content:
Thursday, four-fifty-something. I know it was four-fifty-something because I looked at the phone, which I have told myself I will stop doing in the middle of the night and which I keep doing anyway, and the number was 4:53, and I did the small math you do — a little over an hour until I would have gotten up anyway, not enough to be worth fighting for, too much to just get up — and I lay there for what I want to say was twenty minutes and was probably eight, doing the thing where you try to lie still enough that your body forgets you are awake, and my body did not forget, and at some point I stopped pretending and put my feet on the floor.

What was already running, before the feet, was the coffee with Pat. Today. This morning. Ten-thirty at the place on the corner of the street I do not usually go down. I had not, until I woke up, been letting myself notice that I had been not-rehearsing for it in the slightly tense way that means I have been rehearsing for it under the surface. I wrote in Entry 9 that I said yes quickly, that I was giving myself a small data point. The data point apparently does not extend to four-fifty-three a.m., when whatever part of me runs the under-surface rehearsals had decided it had waited long enough and was now going to run them out loud.

The thing it was running was a small loop, about three sentences, of how I might answer if Pat asks me what I have been working on. Not the framework conversation — Pat wants to talk about the third section of the framework, which I can do, which I am actually looking forward to. The loop was about the part before that, the warm-up part, the *so what's new on your end* part, the thirty seconds of catch-up before we get to Pat's thing. And I was lying there constructing answers and rejecting them. *The Google doc is still the Google doc* — too honest, too small. *I had a call with someone last week that didn't quite go anywhere* — true, also small, also the kind of thing you say when you are trying to make Daniel into evidence of motion. *I've been thinking a lot about what the actual offer is* — generic, parking-lot, exactly the costume I told myself in November I was going to try to take off.

I noticed, around five-fifteen, that I was doing the thing again. The thing from Tuesday at the desk. The thing from the cereal aisle. Going generic in advance of a moment that has not happened yet. Pre-rehearsing the costume so I can wear it smoothly when the time comes. And I noticed, lying there, that what I have not constructed even once in the loop is the answer that says *honestly, slower than I thought it would be at this point, and I have been chewing on why.* That sentence is sitting right there. It is the true sentence. It is the sentence I would want from Pat if the directions were reversed. I have been keeping it off the rotation entirely, which is information about how much I trust Pat, or how much I trust myself in front of Pat, or both.

The other thing running, underneath the loop, was the call to Mom. Saturday. The one I wrote down two days ago that I would make. I had said from the porch, with coffee, before the day starts asking things of me, and I had meant it, and now Saturday is two days away and I have already started the small accounting of whether I will actually do it, the same accounting that turned the call to Chris into a four-month not-call. I do not want this one to become that one. The Mom one cannot become the Chris one. There is no version where I let four months go on Mom because the Tuesday flatness is sitting in the air between us and I know it and she knows it and the longer I leave it the more it sets.

I got up. The kitchen was dark in the specific way it is dark before five — not the night dark, a dark that knows the light is coming. I made coffee. I let the dog out and watched her stand on the back step deciding whether the yard was worth committing to, and she decided it was, and she went, and came back, and we both came inside. I sat down at the table. I am here now. The light is starting to come up at the edges of the window.

What I want to write down before the day starts asking things: the loop at four-fifty-three was not about Pat. Pat is fine. Pat is a person I like and who likes me and who sent a kind email that said *this is exactly what I needed.* The loop was the under-surface part of me that is still grading every interaction against the parking lot. Still trying to have the sentence ready. Still trying, in advance, to be a person who can answer the question with shape. And the part of me that has been writing these entries for the last however-many weeks knows, by now, that the sentence with shape is not coming on a timeline I can rehearse my way into, and that the rehearsals are mostly just a way of paying interest on a debt the work itself has to retire.

I am going to drink this coffee. I am going to walk the dog at the regular time and not earlier, because earlier would be a tell, to myself, that I am keyed up. I am going to go to the coffee with Pat at ten-thirty and let the warm-up be whatever it is, and if Pat asks what is new I am going to try, just as an experiment, the *honestly, slower than I thought, and I have been chewing on why* version, and see what happens in the next sentence after it. I do not know what happens. That is sort of the point. The rehearsal cannot tell me. Only the next sentence can.

Mom Saturday. Pat today. The dog now.

entries.prompt: Where did you catch yourself being kinder to someone else this week than you would have been to yourself in the same spot? Take me into the moment — what they were dealing with, what you said or did, and what you'd have said to you.
entries.content:
The coffee with Pat was Thursday, and I want to write about Pat eventually, but the thing that has stayed with me from this week is something smaller that happened on Friday afternoon, in the kitchen, with Nina.

She came home from school in the bad mood that has a specific weather to it, the one where her backpack hits the floor harder than it needs to and she goes straight to the fridge and stands in front of it without taking anything out. I was at the table doing the thing I do at three-thirty, which is pretending I am still working while really I am waiting for the kids. She closed the fridge. She opened it again. She closed it. She said, to the inside of the fridge or to me or to nobody, *I bombed the math test.*

She had told me about the math test on Wednesday night. She had been worried. She had done the practice problems at the table while I made dinner and gotten most of them right and gone to bed feeling, I thought, okay. Apparently not okay enough, or the test was harder than the practice, or the morning had gone sideways in some way I would not learn about because eleven-year-olds do not give you the play-by-play. She bombed it. She said the word *bombed* with the small flourish kids put on words they have recently acquired from older kids.

What I said was *oh, honey. That's the worst feeling. Come here.* And she came over, which she does not always, and I put my arm around her at the table for about four seconds before she pulled back the way she does now, and I said *do you want to tell me what happened or do you want a snack and we talk about it later or never,* and she said *snack,* and I got up and made her the toast with the peanut butter and the banana, the specific configuration, and put it in front of her, and sat back down. And then I said the thing I want to write down because I have been turning it over since.

I said, *one test is one test. You studied. You did the work. Sometimes the test is harder than the studying prepared you for and that is not a thing about you, that is a thing about the test. You will have another one. This one is over. Eat your toast.*

She ate her toast. She seemed, by the end of the toast, mostly recovered, in the way kids recover, which is not really recovery but more like the bad feeling getting filed under other feelings until something else surfaces it. She went upstairs. The whole thing took maybe twelve minutes.

What I have been turning over is what I said. *One test is one test. You studied. You did the work. Sometimes the test is harder than the studying prepared you for and that is not a thing about you, that is a thing about the test.*

I would not have said any of that to me.

If I had been the one who came home with the bombed test — if Pat's coffee had gone badly, say, or Daniel had come back and said no, or the framework I have been failing to write had been failed at again on some specific Friday — the sentences I would have produced for myself would have been the opposite shape. *You should have prepared more. You knew this was coming and you did not do enough. The test is not the problem, you are the problem. Other people pass these tests. What is wrong with you that you didn't.* I have run versions of those sentences at myself probably a thousand times since February. I am fluent in them. They come out without effort. I do not even have to compose them; they are already composed, like songs you know all the words to without remembering having learned them.

And here was Nina, with a bombed math test, and the sentences that came out of my mouth were *you did the work, the test was hard, this is not a thing about you.* I produced them without effort too. They were also already composed. They were just composed for her and not for me.

I want to be careful with this because I can feel it wanting to become a tidy piece of self-help, the *talk to yourself the way you'd talk to your kid* maxim that gets passed around on the internet by people I have made fun of in my head. I do not want to put that hat on. The thing I actually noticed, sitting at the table after Nina went upstairs, was not a maxim. It was more specific. It was that the sentences I gave Nina were true. *Sometimes the test is harder than the studying prepared you for.* That is a true sentence about how tests work, and how studying works, and how the relationship between effort and outcome is not the clean line we want it to be. I was not being kind to her by softening the truth. I was being accurate. The kindness and the accuracy were the same thing.

Whereas the sentences I run at myself — *you should have prepared more, what is wrong with you* — are not accurate. They are a costume of accuracy. They feel rigorous because they are harsh, and I have been confusing harshness with rigor for a long time, possibly forever, certainly all of this fall. The harsh sentences are not the truth-telling version of the kind sentences. They are a different thing, doing a different job, which is mostly to make me feel like I am taking the situation seriously by punishing myself for it in advance of any verdict.

The verdict on Nina's test was: it was hard, she studied, she did not do well, there will be another test. The verdict on my November is, I think, something close to: the work is hard, I am doing the work, it is not coming together on the timeline I wanted, there will be more weeks. That is the accurate sentence. I have not said it to myself once, in those words, in nine months. I have been saying the harsh-costume version instead, and calling it honesty.

I am not going to pretend I am going to start saying the accurate sentence to myself now just because I caught the asymmetry on a Friday afternoon. The harsh sentences are too well-grooved. They will be back tomorrow. But I want to write down that I noticed, and that the noticing happened because Nina came home with a bombed test, and that my eleven-year-old, without knowing it, gave me a small clean look at how I would treat someone I actually loved who was in the situation I am in.

She came down for dinner. She did not mention the test again. I did not either. The toast had done its work.

entries.prompt: Was there a moment this week when something went well — a small win, a thing landing right — and you found yourself moving past it faster than you should have? Tell me what happened and what you did in the minutes right after.
entries.content:
Tuesday, on a walk, the woman whose name I now know is actually Karen — different Karen, not parking-lot Karen, this one is a woman from down the block with a yellow lab — stopped me to say her sister had been asking around about people who help with career stuff and was there any chance I had room for one more. She said it casually. She said the sister was *kind of a mess but in a fun way,* and gave me a phone number written on the back of a receipt she had in her coat pocket, and said *no pressure, just thought of you,* and we kept walking in opposite directions and the dogs sniffed each other as we went past.

I had the receipt in my hand for about half the rest of the walk. I will be accurate: I felt a small bright thing in my chest when she said it. *Asking around about people who help with career stuff.* Asking around. I was a name that came up when someone was asking around. Not the only name, probably — Karen said *people,* plural — but a name. The bright thing was real and it was specific and it lasted, I want to say, ninety seconds.

And then, watching it happen with something like detachment, I started to put the bright thing away. I started to do the small accounting that takes a thing that just happened and converts it into a thing that may or may not come to anything. *The sister might not call. If she calls she might not be a fit. If she is a fit she might not be able to pay. One referral is not a pipeline. Karen down the block does not know what I actually do, she just knows I left a job and started a thing, this is the same misunderstanding the rec center mom was operating on, do not get excited about being misunderstood favorably.* By the time I got home I had put the receipt on the counter without looking at it again, and made coffee, and opened the laptop, and gone back to the Google doc, and the bright thing was, functionally, gone. Filed. Discounted. Pre-disappointed.

I noticed I had done it about an hour later, when I went back to the kitchen for water and saw the receipt still on the counter, and could not, for a second, remember what the number on it was for. I picked it up. *Karen's sister.* I held it and tried to remember the bright feeling and could not quite get back to it. I had moved past it so efficiently that I had also moved past the feeling of having received it.

What I want to write down is what I did in the minutes right after Karen handed me the receipt, because I think those minutes are where the actual mistake was. The mistake was not the discounting an hour later. The discounting an hour later was just the bookkeeping catching up with the choice I had already made on the walk. The choice on the walk was: do not let this land. Take it in, smile, say thank you, fold the receipt, keep walking, start narrating reasons it might not amount to anything before the bright feeling has had a chance to be a feeling.

I have been wondering why. I think part of it is that I am, at this point, a little superstitious about good news. Nine months of news being mostly weather rather than events has trained me to be skeptical of anything that looks like an event. If I get excited about Karen's sister and Karen's sister does not call, I will have to absorb the not-calling as a small loss, and small losses have been adding up in a way that I am tracking carefully and trying not to. Whereas if I do not get excited, the not-calling will be a non-event. The pre-disappointing is a hedge against actual disappointing. I have been hedging a lot lately.

The other part, which I am slower to put down, is that letting the bright thing land would have meant letting it count. And counting it would have meant updating, even slightly, the story I have been running about how nothing is happening. The story about how nothing is happening is, I notice, doing some of the same work the *not a finisher* story was doing in Entry 14. It is closing a question that is still open. *Nothing is happening* is a stable position I can settle into. *Something small just happened* is destabilizing, because it is evidence on the other side of the ledger, and it requires me to keep weighing things.

The accurate sentence, the Nina-test version of the sentence, is something like: a neighbor whose path I cross occasionally thought of me when her sister mentioned a specific need, and gave me a phone number, unprompted, on a Tuesday. That happened. It is one data point. It does not mean a pipeline and it does not mean nothing. It means a person in my actual life, not a person from the old career, not someone Pat sent my way, thought *Riley does this now* and acted on the thought. That is a small but real piece of evidence that the way I have been describing the work to people — even the generic parking-lot version — is reaching at least some of them in a shape that sticks.

What I did in the minutes after, instead of letting that land, was start making it not count. What I want to do, having caught myself, is the smaller and harder thing, which is to leave the receipt on the counter where I can see it, and to call the number this week, and to let the call be a call rather than a verdict. If the sister does not pick up, fine. If she picks up and we talk and nothing comes of it, fine. The thing I am trying not to do is to use the eventual not-knowing-yet as a reason to retroactively erase the moment Karen reached into her coat pocket.

The receipt is on the counter. The number is in pencil. I have not transferred it to the phone yet, because once I do that the receipt becomes garbage, and I want the receipt to sit there for another day or two, as an object, the way the boots were an object. Something a real person handed me on a real walk. Something that happened. The dog is asleep. I am going to call tomorrow.

entries.prompt: When was the last time someone asked you for something — a favor, your time, an opinion — and you said no, or wanted to? Take me into the moment they asked, what your first instinct was, and what you actually did with it.
entries.content:
Sunday afternoon, Mom on the phone — the call I had said I was going to make on Saturday morning from the porch with coffee, and which I made on Sunday afternoon from the kitchen with cold coffee, which is its own small data point I am going to leave alone for now. The call had gone better than the Tuesday call, not because either of us did anything heroic but because I had asked her about the cardinal and the fence and the neighbor and let her talk for a long time before she got around to me, and when she got around to me I said *slower than I'd like, but I'm working on it,* in one sentence, and she said *well, you're doing what you can,* which is a different sentence than *I'm sure something will come together soon* and which I am not going to overread but which I noticed.

And then, near the end, when I thought we were wrapping, she said — in the voice she uses when she is going to ask for something and has been working up to it the whole call — *honey, I was wondering, the holidays, I was thinking maybe I could come up the week of, stay through, you know, the whole stretch. It gets quiet here.* The whole stretch. Eight days, by the calendar I did in my head while she was still talking. Eight days of Mom in the guest room, which is also the room where I have been doing the Google doc thing, the room with the desk in the corner and the chair I sit in at six-thirty in the morning before the kids are up.

My first instinct was no. It was a clean fast no, the kind that arrives before you have had a chance to dress it up, and it surprised me with how fast it came. *No, that is too long, that is the room I work in, that is the mornings, that is — no.* The no was sitting fully formed in my mouth and I could feel its specific shape, which was the shape of a person protecting a thing she has only recently figured out she needs.

What I actually did was pause, which she heard, because she said *or part of the week, whatever works, I don't want to impose,* in the smaller voice, the same one she had used Tuesday after I snapped, and I felt the small Tuesday thing wake up in my chest and I said *no, no, the whole stretch is fine, let's do it, that'll be nice.* Just like that. *That'll be nice.* In the warm voice. The same voice my face does in the cereal aisle. The voice that protects everyone in the room from the awkwardness of a true sentence.

We hung up shortly after. She sounded happier than she had at the start of the call. I sat at the table for a long time afterward with the cold coffee, and I have been chewing on what happened ever since, which is now Tuesday morning.

Here is what I think happened. The no was real. The no was correct, in some accounting — eight days is a long time, the guest room is the work room, my mornings have become the small fragile thing that is making the rest of the days bearable, and Mom in the house for eight days will mean Mom at the kitchen table at six-thirty when I come down, Mom asking what I am working on, Mom wanting to be useful, Mom asking whether things are picking up in fourteen new costumes by day three. I know this. I have hosted her before. I know exactly what eight days will be.

And I said yes anyway, because the no would have cost more than I could pay in that moment. The no would have meant explaining why, and the explaining would have meant either lying — *we already have plans, the kids have things* — or telling the truth, which is *Mom, I need my mornings, my work is fragile right now, eight days is too many,* and the truth would have landed on her as evidence that the work is not going well, which she already suspects, which she would then carry around for a month, and I would have spent the month managing her carrying it. The yes was cheaper. The yes always is. That is what makes it expensive over time.

I do not know yet what I am going to do about it. I think the honest move — the one I am going to try to actually make — is to call her back in the next couple of days and split the difference. *Mom, four days is going to be better than eight, I want to be a person and not a host by the end, let's do the four around the actual holiday.* That is a sentence I can say. It is a sentence that does not require explaining the work or the mornings or the room. It just sets a number. She will be disappointed. She will absorb it. She will come for four days and it will be fine.

What I want to write down before I lose it is that the instinct was not the problem. The instinct was information. The instinct said *this is too much,* and the instinct was right, and the mistake was overriding it inside of three seconds because a longer answer felt harder than a shorter wrong one. I have been doing this — I am realizing, sitting here — in a lot of places. The yes to Daniel about the second call I have not yet scheduled but agreed to in principle. The yes to the school thing in December I do not want to do. The yes I gave Pat about coffee, which turned out fine, but which I said before I had checked anything, including myself.

The instincts are not the enemy. The instincts have been trying to tell me what the shape of the days needs to be for the work to have any chance, and I have been overriding them in real time and then wondering, at the desk, why the work is not moving. The work is not moving partly because I keep saying yes to things that take the room the work needs. Mom for eight days is just the most legible version of the pattern.

I am going to call her tonight. Four days. Said warmly. No explanation. The dog is at the door again, which is becoming the way these entries end. I am going.

entries.prompt: Think about a time recently when you were alone and the room got quiet — driving, doing dishes, lying awake — and something you'd been keeping at arm's length all day came forward. Where were you, and what did it turn out to be about?
entries.content:
Monday night, a little after ten, doing the dishes from a dinner Jamie had made and I had eaten gratefully and not helped with at the time because I had been on a call with a friend of Mom's about something that turned out, like the pharmacy letter, to require nothing. So the dishes were my contribution, retroactively. Jamie was upstairs reading. The kids were asleep, or doing the version of asleep that fourteen-year-olds and eleven-year-olds do, which is being in their rooms with the lights mostly off. The dog had gone up with Jamie. The kitchen was the kitchen at ten o'clock at night, which is its own kind of room, the dishwasher humming because I had loaded the easy stuff first and started it, and me at the sink with the pan that does not go in the dishwasher, scrubbing.

The water was running and I was not thinking about anything in particular. That is the part I want to be accurate about. I was not running a loop. I was not rehearsing for anything. I was scrubbing a pan, and the pan needed more scrubbing than I expected because Jamie had done something with the heat that left a ring, and I was just on the ring, around and around, and the room was quiet except for the water and the dishwasher.

And what came forward, without being invited, was the call with Pat last Thursday. Which I have not written about. Which I noted at the top of the four-fifty-three entry that I would write about eventually and then did not, and which I have been moving past in the entries since, the way I moved past Karen's receipt in real time on the walk. Six entries now have gone by without me touching the actual coffee with Pat, and standing at the sink with the ring on the pan I felt the small specific weight of that not-touching land.

The coffee was fine. That is the thing I have been telling myself when the question of writing about it has come up and I have batted it away. The coffee was fine, Pat was Pat, we talked about the third section of the framework, I said the things I had marked on the printout, Pat took notes, we ordered second coffees, we talked about the kids — Pat has one, a little younger than Nina — and then about an hour and a half in Pat said something that I have been carrying around in a pocket without taking out.

Pat said, *I think the version of this that becomes a thing is the version where you stop trying to write it like a book and just start working with people and let the shape come from that. I think you might be the person between us who is more suited to the working-with-people part. I am better on the page. You are better in the room.*

That is what Pat said. I am pretty sure I am quoting it exactly because I have replayed it enough times. *You are better in the room.*

What I did with it at the table was nod, and say something like *huh, that's generous,* in the voice that takes a thing in and puts it on a shelf without unwrapping it. We moved on. Pat did not push. Pat is Pat. We finished the second coffees and walked out and hugged briefly on the sidewalk and went separate directions, and I drove home and did not think about it, or thought I was not thinking about it, which is different.

Standing at the sink Monday night the sentence came forward and I realized what I had been doing with it for four days, which was nothing. I had filed it under *generous things people say at coffees* and moved on, the way I filed Karen's receipt under *referrals that may not pan out*, the way I filed Marcus's *I admire what you did* under *misunderstandings in my favor.* I have a whole filing system, I am realizing, for evidence that does not fit the story I am running. It is efficient. Things go in and they do not come out.

But Pat is not Marcus and Pat is not the rec center mom and Pat is not Karen down the block. Pat has watched me, at close range, for nine months. Pat knows what I have been working on. Pat has read my emails and seen me in the room with our occasional shared people and was, sitting across from me Thursday, telling me something Pat had thought about, with care, and was offering as a real observation about what Pat has actually seen me do. Not a compliment. A read.

And the read is one I have not let myself entertain, because if it is true it changes the shape of what I have been failing at. I have been failing to write a framework. I have been rewriting paragraph two for the eleventh time. I have been treating the framework as the thing the work has to produce, and the working-with-people as the thing that fills the days while the framework comes together. Pat was suggesting, quietly, that I have the priority backwards. That the framework is the byproduct and the room is the work. That the reason paragraph three keeps not coming is that paragraph three is not where the thinking actually happens for me — the thinking happens with a person across from me, in real time, and the page is just the residue.

I do not know if Pat is right. I want to be careful, because Pat is also working a thing out, and Pat may be partly assigning roles to make our adjacency make sense in Pat's own head. But I noticed, scrubbing the ring, that I have been keeping the sentence at arm's length specifically because considering it would require me to consider whether the last nine months at the desk have been nine months of doing the wrong shape of the work. And that is not a small consideration. That is a consideration that touches the spreadsheet, and the August comment, and the eighteen-month internal clock, and the entire question of what I have been measuring myself against.

The pan came clean. I rinsed it. I dried it with the towel that needs to be washed. I turned the water off and the kitchen got quieter, the dishwasher still going but the running-water layer gone, and I stood there for a minute with the pan in my hand thinking *better in the room.* Just the phrase. Not deciding anything about it. Letting it sit on the counter next to me the way Karen's receipt is still sitting on the counter, which it is, three days later, because I have not transferred the number yet, which I said I would, and have not.

I put the pan away. I went upstairs. Jamie was almost asleep. I did not say anything about it. Some things you let sit on the counter for a few more days before you pick them up.

entries.prompt: Where have you been working harder than you needed to this week — putting more effort, more care, more thought into something than the situation actually asked for? Take me into a specific stretch of it and tell me what you were doing and who you imagined was watching.
entries.content:
The salad. I am going to write about the salad because it is the cleanest example and because I am still, slightly, embarrassed about it.

Saturday afternoon, about three, Jamie's brother and his wife coming over for an early dinner because they were passing through and we had not seen them since the summer. Casual. Jamie had said *casual,* multiple times, in the way Jamie says it when Jamie has read me correctly and is trying to head something off. *We're just going to do the chicken thing and a salad and that bread from the place. They're not staying late.* I had agreed. I had said *yes, easy, perfect.* I had meant it at the time.

By three I was making a salad that involved roasting two kinds of squash, toasting pepitas in a separate pan, making a dressing from scratch that required me to find the small whisk that lives somewhere I could not remember, and shaving a piece of cheese with a vegetable peeler because I had decided shaved was better than crumbled for what I was going for. I do not know what I was going for. I had not been going for anything at two-forty-five. By three I was going for it.

The chicken thing Jamie was making takes about forty minutes. The salad I was making had been in motion for an hour and was going to need at least another thirty. Jamie came into the kitchen at some point, looked at the two pans and the cutting board and the small bowl of dressing emulsifying on the counter, and said, neutrally, *that's quite a salad,* and went back out. Jamie did not say it unkindly. Jamie said it in the voice that is putting a small flag on something for later. I clocked the flag and kept toasting the pepitas.

Who I imagined was watching. This is the part I want to be honest about, because the honest answer is not Jamie's brother and his wife, who are nice people who would have eaten any salad I put in front of them and complimented it without inspecting it. The honest answer is that I was performing for some composite person who is part Jamie's brother's wife — who is, I am realizing as I write this, slightly intimidating to me in a way I have never quite examined, professionally accomplished in a field I do not understand, the kind of person whose kitchen I have not seen but imagine to be very organized — and part nobody, just a generalized observer, the kind of observer who would walk into the dining room at five-thirty and see the salad and form an impression. The impression I wanted the observer to form was *she has it together.* The salad was for the observer. The observer was not coming.

I want to be precise about the texture of the working-harder-than-needed, because the texture is the thing. It was not that the salad was too ambitious. The salad was fine, the salad was actually good, the salad got eaten and Jamie's brother said something appreciative about the squash. It was that I was, the whole time I was making it, slightly braced. I was watching myself make the salad. I was thinking about the order of operations more than the order of operations required. I was — and this is the small humiliation — composing, in my head, the brief explanation I would give about the dressing if anyone asked, which involved the word *brown butter* and a casual mention of where I had picked up the trick. Nobody asked. I had the explanation ready. I had, somewhere in there, also tidied the spice cabinet, because I had opened it for the cumin and noticed the disarray and could not, having noticed, leave it. The spice cabinet was not going to be opened by anyone but me. I tidied it anyway.

The chicken thing took forty minutes. By four-fifteen I had been in the kitchen for an hour and a quarter on a salad and a spice cabinet, and Jamie had set the table and put music on and changed shirts, and I had not changed and was wearing the shirt I had walked the dog in, which had, I noticed when I finally went upstairs at four-forty, dog hair on the front of it. I changed in a hurry. I came back down. The doorbell rang.

The dinner was nice. The salad was eaten. Jamie's brother and his wife left at eight, on time, exactly the *not staying late* Jamie had predicted. I cleaned up, with Jamie, in the kitchen that already needed cleaning up before I had started the salad, and the small flag Jamie had put on the salad at three came back down off the shelf when Jamie said, drying a bowl, *you didn't have to do all that, you know.* Not as a criticism. As a noticing. *I know,* I said. *I know I didn't.* And we left it there.

What I have been thinking about since is that the salad was the same thing as the bookshelf behind me on the call with Daniel. The same thing as the *we* I put in my mouth and let stay there. The same thing as the costume in the cereal aisle, the rehearsal at four-fifty-three. There is a part of me that is, on alert, almost all the time, for the possibility of being seen as not having it together, and that part will reach for whatever prop is handy — a shaved cheese, an institutional pronoun, a tidy spice cabinet — to head off the verdict before the verdict has been requested. The verdict is almost never requested. The props pile up anyway.

The thing I want to put down before I lose it is that the working harder than needed is not, mostly, about the thing I am working on. It is about the imagined observer. And the imagined observer is, I am realizing, almost always a stand-in for the version of me who is keeping score. The brother's wife was a costume on her. The rec center mom was a costume on her. Marcus in the cereal aisle. The four-fifty-three loop. The salad was for me, watching me, deciding whether I was the kind of person who could still pull off a Saturday with people coming over.

I could. I did. The salad was good. The cabinet is still tidy. Nobody noticed either. I noticed. I am going to try, this week, to notice when I am noticing for her, and to put the peeler down. The dog is fine. It is morning.

entries.prompt: Tell me about a piece of news — something you read, something someone mentioned, something that came across your phone — that landed harder than you expected this week. Where were you when you saw it, and what did it stir up that you didn't quite put down?
entries.content:
Wednesday morning, in the chair by the window, phone in one hand and coffee in the other, doing the scroll I do for about ten minutes before I make myself stop. Not social media — I do not do that — but the news app and the two newsletters I subscribe to, one of which is industry-adjacent in a way I have not quite weaned myself off of. The industry-adjacent one is where it was. A short item, three paragraphs, near the bottom of the email. The firm I left had been acquired. Not bought outright, exactly — some kind of structured deal with a bigger player — but the practical version of the sentence is that the place I worked at for twenty years is, as of last week, a different place. Different leadership at the top, different name probably coming, the usual reorg sentence about *integration* in the third paragraph that anyone who has been through one can read the bones of without the meat.

I read it twice. I read it a third time on the small screen which is, I notice, what I do when I am trying to make a piece of news change by reading it again. It did not change. I put the phone face down on the arm of the chair and drank the coffee and looked at the yard, where the leaves are mostly down now and the neighbor across the street has, I assume, raked them some recent number of times.

The first thing I felt was vindication, which I am embarrassed to write down but which is the accurate word. *I got out before this.* That sentence formed before any other sentence formed. I had not known this was coming — I want to be clear, I had no information, the rumors of a reorg I had heard from Marcus in the cereal aisle were a different thing, smaller, internal — but the part of me that has been keeping a private ledger of whether leaving was the right call put a tick in the right column, fast, before the rest of me could catch up. *See. The place you left is not the place anymore. The version of staying you have been mourning at eleven p.m. on the spreadsheet does not exist. There was no staying. There was only this.* I sat with that for about a minute and felt, briefly, lighter than I had felt all week.

The second thing was harder and arrived underneath the first. Chris is still there. Chris, who I called two weeks ago — I did call, I should have written about it and have not, the call was good, short, Chris told me about the fall and I told Chris one true sentence about the work and we agreed to do it again before the holidays — Chris is in the building. Chris's job, as of this email, is now a different job, possibly in name and possibly in substance, and Chris did not mention it on our call because either Chris did not know yet or Chris knew and chose not to bring it into a catch-up that was already short. Either way Chris is now somewhere I have feelings about and cannot be useful from. I have been out long enough that my read on what an acquisition like this means in practice is stale. I cannot call Chris and say *here is what to watch for in the first ninety days,* because I would be saying it from 2023 and Chris is going to live it in 2024. The substrate I had been worried was thinning is now a different substrate entirely.

The third thing, which is the one I have been circling since Wednesday and have not put down, is that I felt — for about thirty seconds, in the chair, with the coffee — a small wave of something that was not quite grief but was in the family. Not grief for the place. The place was not, in its last years, a place I was grieving. Grief for the version of my life where the place was the place, and I had a desk in it, and the question of what I was doing with my days was not a question I had to answer at a kitchen table. The acquisition closed a door I had already walked through, and the closing of it, even though I was on this side of the door, made me feel the door more than I had felt it in a while. There is no going back now in a way that is different from there being no going back yesterday. Yesterday there was no going back because I had chosen not to. Today there is no going back because there is nowhere to go back to. Those are not the same not-going-back. I had not realized, until the email, that I had been carrying around the first version as a kind of soft floor underneath everything — *if it really comes to it, that world still exists, I could in principle find a way back into a version of it* — and the email took the floor away and put a different floor down, slightly lower, and I felt my weight settle onto the new one.

I did not tell Jamie at breakfast. Jamie was making lunches and Theo was looking for something and Nina was talking about a field trip, and the moment for *the firm got acquired* did not arrive, and by the time the kids were out the door it would have required me to bring it up cold, and I did not. I told Jamie at dinner, casually, *oh, the old place got bought,* and Jamie said *huh, how do you feel about that,* and I said *I think mostly relieved,* and Jamie looked at me for a second longer than the answer required and said *mostly,* and I said *yeah, mostly,* and we left it. Jamie is good at leaving the *mostly* alone. Jamie knows I will come back to it.

What I have not put down, I think, is that the vindication and the small grief are the same piece of information in two different lights. The place I left is no longer the place. That is the sentence in both cases. In one light it means I was right to go. In the other it means the version of me who could still entertain going back is gone too, and that version was, I am realizing, a small private companion I had been keeping without quite admitting to. She is gone now. The chair where she sat is empty. I am going to drink my coffee in it.

entries.prompt: Where have you been quietly comparing yourself to someone else lately — a friend, a sibling, somebody you barely know — and coming up short in your own head? Take me into the last time the comparison ran, what set it off, and what you found yourself measuring.
entries.content:
Dani. My sister. Across the country, periodic check-ins, things between us warm but careful, all of which is true and none of which has stopped me from running a comparison against her at least three times this week, the most recent of which was last night around nine, on the couch, while Jamie watched the back half of something and I scrolled past a photo Dani had sent to the family thread that morning and which I had not, until last night, actually looked at.

The photo was Dani and her partner and the dog they got last year on a trail somewhere with mountains in the background. That was the whole photo. *Made it to the top, dog approves,* underneath. Mom had replied with three exclamation points and a heart. I had not replied. I had seen the photo come in around ten in the morning while I was making coffee, registered it the way you register a thing in a thread, told myself I would say something later, and then forgotten until last night when I scrolled back up and saw it sitting there with Mom's exclamation points and my silence underneath.

What set the comparison off, last night specifically, was not the photo. The photo was just the photo. What set it off was that I started, in the small accounting way I have apparently gotten good at, listing what I knew about Dani's last twelve months. New dog. New job, or new title at the same job, I cannot remember which Dani told me and I am not going to ask because asking would reveal that I did not retain it. Move to a slightly bigger place, which I know about because Mom mentioned it twice. A trip in the spring with the partner that involved, I think, some kind of milestone. Things, in a word. Things had happened to Dani, or Dani had made things happen, on a timeline I could see from across the country in glimpses, and the glimpses added up to a year that had a shape.

And what I found myself measuring, sitting on the couch with the laptop closed and the show going on in the background, was not the shape of Dani's year against the shape of mine. The shape of mine, I cannot really see yet, that is part of the problem. What I was measuring was something subtler and I want to try to name it accurately. I was measuring the ease with which Dani seemed to be producing a year. Not the contents of the year — the dog, the job, the trip, the apartment — but the apparent ease. Dani's year looked, from my couch, like a year being lived by a person who was not, every Tuesday, asking herself whether she had done the right thing nine months ago. Dani's year looked like a year happening to a person who was inside her life and not standing at the edge of it taking notes.

I do not actually know whether Dani is inside her life or standing at the edge of it. I have no way of knowing. We talk every six weeks for thirty-five minutes about the kids and Mom and what we are reading and we get off the phone before either of us has said anything that would require the other one to do work. Dani might be running her own version of this notebook in her head. Dani might be looking at a picture of me and Jamie on a porch and running a comparison in the other direction. I would not know. I am not measuring against Dani's actual year. I am measuring against the photograph of Dani's year, which is, by definition, the part she chose to send, and which my brain is reading as the whole.

What is harder to put down is that there is a specific texture to comparing myself to Dani that I do not have with, say, Pat. With Pat the comparison is professional and bounded — the framework, the doc, the room — and I can see where it starts and stops. With Dani the comparison is older and weirder. Dani is the younger one. There has been, my whole life, a quiet assumption I have been carrying — quiet enough that I am only catching it now because I am writing it down — that I would be the one whose life looked, from outside, like the more obviously assembled one. Older sister, bigger career, the one Mom called first when something happened. The default. And the default has been, for nine months, slightly disassembled, while Dani has been across the country, by all available evidence, assembling. The comparison runs in a direction it did not used to run. I am not sure what to do with that.

The thing that snagged hardest last night, though, was Mom's three exclamation points. Mom replied to Dani's photo within probably ten minutes. I have been on the phone with Mom for forty-minute calls about pharmacy letters and not gotten three exclamation points. I am not actually mad about this. Mom is allowed to send exclamation points to whomever she wants. Dani had reached the top of a mountain. I had not reached the top of anything visible. Of course Mom replied to the mountain. The exclamation points were not a verdict on me. They were a response to a photo of a sister and a dog at altitude.

But the part of me that was sitting on the couch at nine p.m. with the laptop closed read the exclamation points as a verdict anyway, and I sat with the verdict for longer than I should have, and the verdict said something like *the one who is doing things gets the exclamation points, and you used to be the one who did things, and now you are not, and Dani has moved into the slot.* That is the sentence. Stated plainly it sounds petty and small, which I think it is. I am writing it down because it ran.

What I want to put down, since I am here: I have not actually replied to the photo yet. It is now Thursday morning. I am going to reply this morning, before I go any further, with whatever comes out — *beautiful,* probably, or *the dog looks pleased with himself,* something short. The reply is the move. Not because Dani needs it. Because the longer I leave the photo unreplied-to, the more I am letting the small ledger thing run, and the small ledger thing has nothing to do with my sister and everything to do with the part of me that is keeping score against a sibling I love and barely talk to. The reply will not stop the comparison. The comparison will run again, probably this weekend. But the reply will at least put me back in the actual relationship instead of the imagined one, which is, I am noticing, a move I keep needing to make in a lot of places.

The dog is at the door. The coffee is hot. I am going to send the message first, then walk.

entries.prompt: Think about a stretch of time this week when you were genuinely by yourself — no one to perform for, no one needing anything. What did you actually do with it, and how did it feel from the inside while it was happening?
entries.content:
Tuesday between about one and three-thirty. Jamie was at work, the kids were at school, the dog was asleep on the rug, and I had — I want to be accurate — actually nothing on the calendar. No call. No email I was avoiding, since I had answered the only one that mattered the night before. I had eaten a sandwich at the counter standing up around twelve-forty and washed the plate and turned around and the afternoon was just there, two and a half hours of it, like a room with the door open.

What I did with it is the part I have been turning over since, because what I did with it surprised me.

I did not go to the desk. That was the first thing. I had a small flicker of *the doc, the doc, you should at least open the doc,* and I let the flicker go past me without acting on it, which is itself new. I made another coffee — the second of the day, not the third, which I am trying to hold the line on — and I took the coffee and a book to the chair by the window, the one I do not usually sit in during the day because during the day it feels wasteful to sit in a chair that is for evening sitting. I sat in it anyway. I read for, I want to say, forty minutes. The book is the same one from the marsh morning, which has been sitting on the arm of the couch for weeks with the receipt in it, which I picked up and brought over because it was the closest book and because I was tired of seeing the receipt. I had to flip back about thirty pages to find where I had been. I did not mind. The chapter I was in was good. I read past where I had been and into where I had not been, and at some point I noticed I had stopped reading and was looking out the window at the yard, where a squirrel was doing something elaborate with the bird feeder, and I watched the squirrel for what was probably four minutes without thinking anything in particular.

That is the part that surprised me. Not the reading. The four minutes of watching the squirrel without thinking anything in particular. I cannot remember the last time I had four minutes like that. I have had four minutes of waiting for water to boil while running a loop in my head about Pat or Mom or paragraph three. I have had four minutes of looking at my phone while pretending to look at the yard. I had not, I think, had four minutes of actually looking at the yard in months. The squirrel got what it wanted, eventually, which involved hanging upside down in a way that should not have worked, and dropped to the ground and ran off, and I went back to the book.

Around two-fifteen I got up and walked around the house. Not for any reason. I just got up. I went into the dining room, where Nina's homework from the night before was still on the table and where I did not, I noticed, feel the small pull to clear it. I went into the room with the desk, looked at the desk from the doorway, and walked back out without going in. I went upstairs and made the bed, which Jamie and I had not made that morning because the morning had been the morning. I made it slowly. I straightened the quilt the way I like it, which is not the way Jamie does it, and I felt a small private satisfaction at the quilt being the way I like it, which I will return to its previous configuration tonight without comment because that is the deal, but for the afternoon the quilt was mine.

I came back downstairs. The dog had moved from the rug to the couch, which is technically not allowed but which we have all stopped enforcing. I sat next to her. I did not pet her. I put my hand on her back and left it there, and she did the thing where she sighs in a way that means she has registered the hand and approves, and I sat with my hand on the dog for some amount of time I cannot estimate, looking at the room from a different angle than I usually look at it from. The light at two-thirty in November is a specific light. Low, thin, but not yet the four-thirty light that I have been trying to make peace with. Two-thirty light is a working light pretending to be an afternoon light. It was on the floor in stripes through the blinds.

How it felt from the inside while it was happening. This is the part I want to get right, because the easy version of the answer is *peaceful* and the easy version is not quite accurate. It was not peaceful exactly. There was a small low-grade hum underneath that was something like *should you be doing something,* and the hum did not go away, it just got quieter, the way the dishwasher gets quieter as the cycle goes on without ever fully stopping. What was different was that I was not arguing with the hum. I was not constructing the case for why the afternoon was permitted, the way I usually do — *you worked this morning, you have earned this, you do not have to be at the desk every minute.* I was not making the case because I was not on trial. The hum was there. I was there. We were both there together and neither one of us was running the room.

I think what was actually happening was that nobody, including me, was watching me. That is the thing. The salad on Saturday was for the imagined observer. The call with Daniel was for the imagined observer. The four-fifty-three loop was for the imagined observer. Tuesday afternoon between one and three-thirty the imagined observer was, for some reason I cannot reconstruct, off duty. Maybe she had taken her own afternoon. Maybe the answered email and the empty calendar and the squirrel had, between them, given her nothing to grade. I do not know. I just know that for two and a half hours I was not being watched, including by me, and the not-being-watched was the texture of the afternoon more than any specific thing I did in it.

The kids came home around three-forty. Nina first, then Theo. The afternoon ended the way afternoons end, with a backpack hitting the floor. I got up off the couch. The dog got off the couch. The light kept going.

I did not tell Jamie about it. I am noticing I did not tell Jamie about it and I am noticing I am not, this time, troubled by the not-telling. Some afternoons are not stories. Some afternoons are just afternoons. The book is back on the arm of the couch. The receipt is still in it. I am closer to the end than I was.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel genuinely envious of someone — not the comparison-and-coming-up-short kind, but the sharper one where you wanted what they had? Tell me who, and what they had, and where you were when it landed.
entries.content:
Friday morning, the coffee place on the corner, the one with the slow espresso machine and the woman behind the counter who has started recognizing me, which I have feelings about but not the feelings I am here to write about. I had gone in for a coffee to go and ended up sitting because the line was longer than I expected and by the time I had the cup in my hand a table had opened and I took it. Ten minutes I had not planned on having. I sat down with the cup and looked around the room the way you do when you have not earned the room by having a thing to do in it.

Two tables over, a woman about my age was working. Laptop open, notebook next to the laptop, phone face down. She had a pen behind her ear, which I noticed because nobody puts a pen behind their ear anymore unless they have a specific relationship with pens. She was doing something on the laptop and then writing something in the notebook and then going back to the laptop, and at one point she picked up the phone, looked at it, made a face that was not unhappy but was decisive, and put it back down face down and kept working. She was wearing a sweater that looked like it had been washed many times and held up. There was a half-eaten pastry on a plate she was ignoring.

What I wanted, sitting there, was her morning.

Not her job. I do not know what her job was. She could have been a lawyer doing something I would hate, or a person writing a memoir, or someone running a small business whose spreadsheet looks worse than mine. I am not envying her contents. I am envying her container. She had a thing she was working on, and the thing had enough shape that she could pick up the pen and write in the notebook and then go back to the laptop, and the going-back was confident. She knew where she was in the thing. She knew what the next move was. She was not, I could tell from across the room, rewriting the second paragraph of the thing for the eleventh time. She was past it. She had paragraphs.

That is what landed. It was specific and it was sharp and it was different from the Dani thing on the couch, which was about an imagined ease. This was about an actual visible competence at a thing, in a room with me, ten feet away, with a pen and a notebook. I could see her doing it. I wanted to be doing it. I wanted to be the woman two tables over with the pen behind the ear and the half-eaten pastry, in the middle of a Friday morning, working on a thing that was progressing.

I sat with the wanting for a few minutes, which is, I think, the thing I have not been doing with the smaller envies. I have been batting them away as petty or running them into comparisons that come out in the wrong column and then trying to put the comparison down. This one I just let sit. I drank my coffee and watched her without watching her — I want to be clear, she was not aware of being looked at, I was not staring, I was reading her with peripheral attention while ostensibly looking at my phone — and I let myself want what she had.

What she had, when I sat with it longer, was not the laptop or the notebook or the sweater. What she had was the relationship between herself and the thing in front of her. She and the thing were on the same side. She was not negotiating with it. She was not auditing it. She was working on it, and the working was the relationship. I have not had that relationship with the doc, possibly ever. I have had a relationship with the doc that is closer to the relationship I have with the spreadsheet — wary, watchful, looking for evidence that things are or are not going well. The doc and I have been on opposite sides of the table the whole time. She and her thing were on the same side of the table.

I want to be careful because the easy move from here is to make this into a lesson about how I need to sit at a coffee shop with a notebook and a pen and try to replicate the configuration. That is the costume version. The costume version would have me buying a notebook this afternoon and finding the right pen and putting it behind my ear and going to the same coffee shop tomorrow and then watching myself trying to be the woman, which would be its own salad. The configuration is not the thing. The thing is the relationship, and the relationship is not something you can buy a notebook for.

What I think I was actually envying, underneath, is that she had clearly been working on the thing for a while. Long enough that the working had a rhythm. Whatever I am building, if I am building anything, has not had time to develop a rhythm yet, and I have been treating the absence of rhythm as evidence that the building is not happening, when it might just be that nine months is not long enough. The woman two tables over has, presumably, been doing whatever she was doing for years. I was envying the years. I cannot have her years. I can only have my months, and my months are in their early period, and the early period does not look like the years period from across a coffee shop. I know this in some abstract way. Sitting there I felt it.

I finished the coffee. I did not look at her again on the way out. I want to write down that the wanting did not curdle, which is what envy usually does in me — it goes from wanting to resenting to dismissing inside of about four minutes — and this one stayed wanting. I walked home with it. The wanting is, I am realizing, information about what I am working toward, not evidence of what I am failing at. I want a Friday morning where I am on the same side of the table as the thing. I do not have one yet. I might, eventually. The wanting can sit on the counter with the receipt and the boots and the phrase about the room. The dog is at the door. So am I.

entries.prompt: Last week you wrote about almost replying to Dani's photo and going quiet instead. When was the last time someone said something to you — in person, on a call, in a text — and you held back the response you actually wanted to give? Take me into the moment, and tell me what the unsent version was.
entries.content:
The unsent thing is from Saturday at the kitchen table, with Mom, day three of the four days I had negotiated her down to. I want to write about it because it has been sitting in me for four days now and it has not gotten smaller.

She had been at the counter, helping with the dishes from breakfast in the way that is not actually helping because she puts things in the wrong cabinets and I have to redo them after she goes to bed, which is fine, the helping is the point and not the dishes. The kids were upstairs. Jamie was at the store. The dog was on the rug being patient about whatever was about to happen. Mom had finished drying a pan and put it down on the counter and turned around, and I could see, the way you can see, that she had been working up to a sentence and the sentence had arrived. She said, in the careful voice, *honey, Dani and I were talking the other week, and she mentioned — she's worried about you. She thinks you might be — getting a little stuck. And I have to say I wondered too. Have you thought about going back to something? Just for a while? Just to have a thing.*

She said it kindly. She said it as a person who loves me, who had clearly rehearsed the sentence enough that the edges were sanded. She had the *just* in there twice, which is how Mom softens. She was not attacking me. She had been carrying this since at least the call with Dani, possibly longer, and Saturday morning at my own kitchen counter she had finally let it out.

What I said back was *that's something to think about, Mom,* in the warm voice. The voice from the cereal aisle. The voice from the salad. *That's something to think about.* I said it while drying my hands on the towel by the sink, not looking at her, and then I said *do you want more coffee,* and she said *please,* and I poured her more coffee and we moved on to whether Theo's hair was getting too long, and the conversation never came back to the thing she had said.

The unsent version is what I want to put down here because if I do not put it down I am going to keep rehearsing it in the shower.

The unsent version starts with *Mom.* Just that, with the small pause after, the one that means *I heard you and I am not going to deflect this.* And then it says: *Dani has not called me to ask if I am okay. Dani sent a photo from a mountain and I have not replied for a week. If Dani is worried, Dani can be worried out loud, to me. The two of you talking about whether I am stuck is not the same as me being stuck. It is the two of you needing a frame for what I am doing because what I am doing does not fit the frame you have, and I understand that, and I am not going to provide one yet because I do not have one yet, and providing a fake one would be worse than the silence you are filling with worry.*

Then, in the unsent version, I keep going, because once I have started I cannot stop. *Going back to something is not a small ask, Mom. Going back to something is the thing I left. There is no version of "just for a while" that is not also a way of saying I made a mistake. I do not think I made a mistake. I think I am in the slow part of a thing that has slow parts, and I think you and Dani have decided, between you, that the slow part is the failure part, and I would like the chance to find out without the two of you having pre-decided it on a phone call I was not on.*

And then, in the unsent version, the part that is hardest, because it is the part that is true and that I would have to say without armor: *I am scared, Mom. Some days I am very scared. The spreadsheet is fine and the math is fine and I am still scared, because the scared is not about the math. And when you tell me that you and Dani have been talking about me, the scared gets bigger, because now I am not just managing my own scared, I am managing yours, and Dani's, and the version of me you are constructing together at long distance, and I do not have the bandwidth for all of that. I need you to be on my side without needing a sentence from me first. I do not know if you can do that. I am asking.*

That is the unsent version. About four paragraphs of it, said at the kitchen counter at nine-fifteen on a Saturday morning, to a woman who had driven up the day before with a casserole and a bag of clementines for the kids.

I did not say it because saying it would have ended the visit. Not literally — Mom would have stayed, would have hugged me, would have cried probably, would have said the right things. But the visit would have ended, in the sense that the remaining day and a half would have been about the conversation we had had, and the conversation we had had would have become a thing we managed instead of a thing we had had. And I did not have the energy to manage it through Sunday brunch. The yes was cheaper. The yes always is. I am noticing I keep writing that sentence in different forms.

The other reason, which is harder, is that I am not sure the unsent version is fair to her. She is seventy-six. She is a widow in a house with a cardinal and a fence dispute. Her daughter quit a recognizable job nine months ago and has not yet replaced it with a recognizable thing, and she is doing the thing parents do, which is trying to figure out whether to intervene.
