The intervention came out clumsy. It also came out, and I do not want to deny this, from love. The unsent version is fair to me. I am not sure it is fair to her. I do not know what I owe her in that gap.

She left Sunday afternoon. I hugged her at the car for a beat longer than usual. I did not say *I am scared* and I did not say *do not talk to Dani about me.* I said *drive safe, call when you get home.* She did. I am going to call her this week, before the next call she calls me on, and I am going to try to say one true sentence — not all four paragraphs, one — and see what happens in the sentence after it. The dog is at the door. I am going.

entries.prompt: Was there a small thing this week that made you laugh out loud, or come close to it? Take me into the moment — where you were, what set it off, and what the laugh felt like on the way out.
entries.content:
Wednesday morning, in the laundry room, which is not a room so much as a closet with a door that does not fully close, and which is where the dog has decided, in the last two weeks, that the warm spot in front of the dryer is the best spot in the house. I had gone in to switch over a load and she had followed me, the way she does when there is any chance the activity might turn out to involve her, and she had wedged herself between my legs and the dryer in the small triangular space that exists there, and was looking up at me with the specific expression she has when she is convinced something is about to happen for her benefit.

I opened the dryer. I started pulling the warm clothes out into the basket on the floor, and I was on autopilot, not really paying attention, and I pulled out a pair of Jamie's socks balled together and a t-shirt and then a thing that I could not, for a second, identify. Some kind of small dense knotted object. I held it up. It was the dog's bandana, the navy one with the small white pattern that she wears for, I want to say, no reason — Jamie put it on her once around a holiday and we never took it off, and it has been on her or near her for what is probably eight months. It must have come off in the yard. It must have gotten picked up with a towel. It had been through the wash and the dryer. It was clean and warm and slightly smaller than it had been.

I held it up and the dog saw it and her ears did the thing, the small lift, the *that is mine,* and I said, in the voice I use with her that I would never use in front of another adult, *did you go through the dryer,* and she wagged once, the single wag that means *yes to whatever you just said,* and I started laughing.

Not the honking laugh from the pizza car. A smaller laugh, but the same family. The kind that starts in the chest as a small heave and comes out as a sound that is not really a word. I was standing in front of the dryer with a basket of warm laundry at my feet and a clean bandana in my hand and the dog wedged against my leg looking pleased with herself, and the laugh kept going for, I want to say, fifteen seconds, which is a long time to laugh alone in a laundry room about a piece of fabric.

What set it off, I think, was not the bandana itself. The bandana was the trigger. What set it off was the small ridiculous fact of the bandana having had its own little adventure — gotten lost, gotten swept up, gotten tumbled, come out clean and warm and ready to go back on the dog, who had been looking for it for some indeterminate number of days without knowing she was looking for it. The whole sequence of events had happened entirely without me. The bandana had been in the yard, then in the laundry pile, then in the washer, then in the dryer, and at no point had I been the agent of any of it. I had just opened the dryer and there it was. The bandana had had a week. I had not given it the week. The week had just happened to it.

I put it on her. She held still for it, which she does only for things she likes, and I tied it the way Jamie ties it, which is looser than I would tie it, and stood up, and she shook herself the way dogs do when they have been adjusted, and trotted out of the laundry room with the bandana on, and I was still half-laughing, picking up the basket.

What the laugh felt like on the way out — I want to try to describe this because it is different from the pizza car laugh, which was social and which was about Theo coming back to us and which I felt mostly in my face and chest. The laundry room laugh was lower and quieter and was, I think, mostly relief. Not relief about anything in particular. Relief about the bandana being clean. Relief that the small machinery of the household had, while I was not paying attention, produced a small comic outcome that did not require my management. The bandana was a thing that had fixed itself. So many things in the last nine months have required my management, including things that do not actually need me to manage them but which I manage anyway because I am here and the management is what I have to offer in the absence of a clearer thing. The bandana had not asked. The bandana had just gone on its small bandana journey and come out the other side warm and ready, and the dog was wearing it again, and the household was, briefly, a small functioning system that had handled itself.

I carried the basket into the bedroom and started folding. Jamie's socks. Theo's t-shirts, which keep getting bigger in a way that startles me when I am folding them. Nina's leggings, which are at the stage where the holes in the knees are, apparently, on purpose. The dog came in and lay down on the bed in violation of the rules nobody enforces, the bandana still on, the small white pattern catching the light from the window. I caught my own face in the mirror over the dresser doing a small smile at no one, and felt, in a way I am wary of saying out loud, like a person who lives somewhere. Not a person between things. A person folding laundry on a Wednesday in a house with a dog wearing a bandana that had had a more eventful week than I had.

The smile stayed for a while. I want to write that down. The bandana is back on the dog. The dryer is still going with the next load. I am going to make more coffee, which will be the third, and I am going to be okay about it.

entries.prompt: Why did you say yes to four days with your mom instead of the number you actually wanted? Go back to the moment the number got decided — on the phone, in your head, wherever it happened — and tell me what was running through you as you landed on four.
entries.content:
The number was three.

I want to put that down first, before the rest of it, because I have been telling myself the number was four. Four was the number I said out loud, and four was the number I called her back with, and four is the number that has now been used and gone, and in the small bookkeeping I have been doing about whether I held the line on the call I told myself I would make in Entry 20, four has been counted as a win. *I asked for less. I split the difference. I did the harder thing.* All true, in a kind of way. Also a small dishonesty I am noticing now, scrubbing the inside of the coffee pot at seven a.m. with the dog watching, which is where this question apparently caught up with me.

The number was three. Three is what I had in my head when I picked up the phone that Tuesday night to call her back. Three was clean. Three was Friday afternoon to Monday morning — arrive for dinner, two full days, leave before the week starts. Three did not eat the mornings on either end. Three meant the room with the desk would be hers for two nights and mine again on Monday, when the doc would still be open from where I had left it. I had walked through the days in my head while I was loading the dishwasher before the call. Three.

What was running through me when I dialed was already the negotiation with three. I want to be accurate about this because I think this is the thing. I had not even said three to her yet and I was already preparing to lose it. The dialing was the start of the climb-down. I was thinking about her face — not on the phone, the imagined face, the one she makes when she absorbs disappointment by getting smaller — and I was thinking about the silence after I would say three, and I was running the math on what three would cost her, which I had decided, before she had picked up, was more than what eight would cost me.

That is the sentence. I had decided, in advance, that her disappointment was the heavier currency. I had not weighed it against anything. I had just decided.

She picked up on the second ring. She sounded happy I was calling, which she always does, which I notice now is itself a piece of information I have been factoring in without naming. Mom is happy when I call. The bar for that happiness is low. The cost of denting it feels, every time, higher than it should. I said *Mom, I was thinking about the holidays,* and she said *yes, honey,* in the warm voice, and I felt three start to slip.

The slip was not a moment. The slip was the whole next sentence. I heard myself say *I was thinking maybe instead of the whole stretch we do something a little shorter,* and I heard, in the space after *shorter,* my own brain auditioning numbers. Three. Four. Five. Three was correct. Three was the answer. Four was the answer plus a tip. Five was the answer plus a tip plus an apology. I went with four. *Maybe four days, around the actual holiday, so we get the good part without anyone getting tired.* I added the *anyone getting tired* because it spread the cost across both of us, made it about pacing instead of about my mornings, made it a logistical sentence instead of a need sentence.

She said *four days sounds lovely, honey,* and the warmth in her voice was the same warmth as for eight, which is the part I want to put down because it is the part I keep not letting myself see. She would have said four sounds lovely. She would have said three sounds lovely. She would have said *whatever works for you* and meant it, in the way she means most things, which is mostly. The eight had been an opening bid. I had treated it as a verdict.

What was running through me as I landed on four — actually running through me, in the second between *shorter* and the number — was a small fast calculation that went something like: *three is the number I want, three is the number that protects the work, four is the number she will not be hurt by, take the four, you can survive four, you cannot survive her being hurt this week on top of everything else.* That calculation took less than a second. It used the word *survive* twice, about a four-day visit from a seventy-six-year-old woman who brings clementines. I am writing that down so I can see it.

The other thing running, underneath, was older. I have been the one who manages her since Dad died, which is going on six years now. Dani is across the country and Dani gets a pass for being across the country, and I have absorbed the pass as the older sister and as the closer one and as the one who can drive an hour. The managing has had its own small budget in my head — calls on Wednesdays, the pharmacy form, the visit at her kitchen table about the letter, the four days at the holidays — and the budget has been one I do not really question, because questioning it would mean having a conversation with Dani about distribution that I do not want to have, and would mean having a conversation with myself about resentment that I do not want to have either. The four was inside the budget. The three would have required me to look at the budget. I did not want to look at the budget on a Tuesday night at nine-fifteen with the dishwasher running.

So I took the four. And four is what we did. And four was, by the way, fine, mostly — the Saturday sentence she said about Dani worrying was not a thing the three would have prevented, the sentence would have come on day two of three the same as it came on day three of four — but the mornings did get eaten, both ends, and the doc did not get opened until Tuesday after she left, and I was tireder than I had expected to be in a way that I told myself was the visit and was, I think now, partly the small steady cost of having said a number I did not mean and then living inside it for ninety-six hours.

What I want to write down before the kids come down is that the negotiation was over before it started. I called her with three in my head and four in my mouth, and the gap between those two was the whole story, and the gap is a gap I have been letting exist in a lot of places without naming it. The instinct in Entry 20 was right. The instinct on the phone Tuesday was also right. The instinct keeps being right. I keep overruling it on a timeline that is shorter than the timeline it would take to even hear what it is saying.

Three would have been fine. She would have come for three. The cardinal would still have been the cardinal. I am going to try, next time, to say the number I mean and let the silence after it do its own work. The dog is at the door. The coffee is the second one already. I am going.

entries.prompt: Tell me about something you've made with your hands recently — a meal, a fix, a small project, anything you built or put together. Take me into the doing of it: what your hands were doing, where your head went while you worked.
entries.content:
The shelf in the laundry room. The one above the dryer that has been holding the box of dryer sheets and the bottle of stain stuff and a roll of paper towels and an unidentified small basket that I think used to hold clothespins, and which has been, since we moved into this house eleven years ago, very slightly tilted toward the left. Not enough that things slide off. Enough that you can see it, if you are looking at it, and I had stopped looking at it for probably nine of those eleven years and started looking at it again sometime this fall, possibly because I have been in the laundry room more, possibly because of the bandana incident which I notice has made the laundry room into a place I am now slightly fond of, but for whatever reason the tilt had become something I could see every time I went in there, and on Saturday morning I decided to fix it.

I want to be honest that the deciding was, itself, a small evasion. I had told myself I was going to spend Saturday morning at the desk. The doc has been opened most days this week, which is new, and the new-ness has a small fragile quality I am trying not to disturb, and Saturday morning was supposed to be a continuation of that. Instead I came downstairs, made coffee, walked past the desk, went into the laundry room to switch over a load, looked at the tilt, and thought *today.* I knew, I think, that *today* was a way of not going to the desk. I went anyway. The tilt had been waiting eleven years. The desk could wait an hour.

What it required: a screwdriver, a level — which I had to find, which took about fifteen minutes of looking in three drawers and the basement and finally a hook in the garage Jamie had put it on at some point without telling me, or telling me and me forgetting — and the willingness to take everything off the shelf. I took everything off the shelf. The dryer sheets, the stain stuff, the paper towels, the basket, which turned out to be holding, underneath a layer of dust, a single child's mitten of a kind neither of my children has worn for at least six years. I held the mitten for a second. I put it on top of the washer to deal with later, which is a category I have for objects that turn up during other tasks, and which usually means the object will sit there for two weeks before being thrown out or, occasionally, returned to circulation.

The shelf came off the brackets easier than I expected. The brackets were the problem. The left one was set about a quarter inch lower than the right one — I do not know how, I do not know if it was that way when we moved in or whether it had migrated, brackets do not usually migrate — and the fix was to unscrew it, find the stud again, move it up, and rescrew. Simple. Twenty minutes, maybe. Including the level-finding, an hour and change.

What my hands were doing was the thing I want to write about. They were doing the thing hands do when they have done a lot of small fixes over the years and have a memory of their own about how to do them. My hands knew, without being told, to put the screws in the cup of the level so they would not roll off. My hands knew to test the bracket with one finger before letting go. My hands knew the small backward turn at the end of tightening a screw to keep it from stripping. None of this was conscious. I was not narrating any of it to myself. My hands were just doing what they do, and I was, somewhere above them, watching.

Where my head went. This is the part I want to be accurate about. My head, for the first ten minutes, was on the doc. It was running the small version of the loop it has been running all week — paragraph three, the framing, whether the thing Pat said about the room is changing what paragraph three should even be — and I was vaguely irritated with myself for being in the laundry room with a screwdriver instead of at the desk where the loop was at least productive in an ambient way. Around the time I had the bracket off and was holding it against the wall to mark the new screw holes, the loop got quieter. Not gone. Quieter. By the time I was driving the new screws in — slow, by hand, because power tools in this house live in a specific bin in the basement and I was not going to go get them — the loop had stopped. My head was just on the screws.

And what came in, to fill the space the loop had been taking, was nothing in particular. Some thoughts about the mitten on the washer. A thought about the squirrel from Tuesday, which I had not thought about since Tuesday. A thought about whether we had eggs. Then a longer thought, which surfaced while I was holding the level against the bracket and watching the bubble, about the fact that I was good at this. Not great. Not a person who should be doing other people's shelves. But competent. The shelf was going to be level and was going to stay level, because I knew, in my hands and in some quiet older part of my head, how to make a shelf level. I had not learned it for the new work. I had not learned it for the old work. I had just, over the years, learned it, for myself, because shelves had needed to be level and nobody else was going to do them. There was a small unflashy pleasure in noticing this.

I put the shelf back on. I put the things back on the shelf. The basket, without the mitten. The paper towels. The stain stuff. The dryer sheets. I stepped back. The shelf was level. The room looked the same to anyone but me. I knew. I stood there for a second with the screwdriver still in my hand and felt, in a small clean way, that I had finished a thing. Not a big thing. A thing. The whole thing took about an hour and twenty minutes including the level hunt, and at the end of it there was an object in the world that was different than it had been at the beginning, and the difference was visible, and was mine, and was done. The doc had not moved. The shelf had. Some Saturdays that is the trade. The mitten is still on the washer.

entries.prompt: Where did frustration show up this week in a way that felt out of proportion to what set it off? Take me to the moment it spiked — what was actually happening, and what you did with it in the next few minutes.
entries.content:
Thursday afternoon, around three, in the car in the parking lot of the grocery store, before I had even gone in. I want to be accurate about where it spiked because the spike happened before any groceries, before any cereal aisle, before any human being asked anything of me. I was sitting in the car with the engine off and my hand on the door handle and I could not, for a second, remember what I had come in for. I had a list on my phone. I knew I had a list on my phone. I could not, in the moment, find the energy to open the phone and look at the list, and the not-finding-the-energy made me angry in a way that was completely out of register with the situation, which was: a woman in a parked car, about to buy groceries.

What had actually happened, in the run-up, was nothing. That is the part I want to put down. Nothing had happened. The morning had been fine. The doc had been open. I had written something like a hundred and fifty words on paragraph three, which is more than I have written on paragraph three in a single sitting in months, and I had felt, getting up from the desk at noon, a small clean version of the shelf feeling. I had eaten a sandwich. I had taken the dog out. I had gotten in the car at two-forty to do the grocery run because we were out of milk and a few other things and I had told Jamie I would get them on the way to picking up Nina from the thing at four. The drive was twelve minutes. The list was six items. I had a window of about forty minutes. There was no problem.

And in the parking lot, with my hand on the door handle, something rose up in my chest that was, when I name it now, fury. Not at anything. At the parking lot, maybe. At the list. At the fact that I was about to spend twenty minutes of a Thursday in a fluorescent-lit room being a person who buys milk, when I had just spent the morning being, briefly, a person who writes paragraphs. The transition from the one to the other was, in the moment, intolerable. I wanted to put the car back in drive and go home. I wanted, for about four seconds, to call Jamie and say *can you do this on your way home,* knowing Jamie could not, knowing Jamie was on the other side of town, knowing the ask was not really an ask but a small loud noise I wanted to make in someone's direction.

I did not call Jamie. I sat in the car. The fury did the thing fury does when it has nothing to land on, which is get bigger for about thirty seconds and then start looking, frantically, for a target. The target it found was the cart corral, because there were carts strewn around it in the way carts get strewn, and I had a small vivid thought about the people who had not put their carts back, and I rode that for maybe ten seconds before I noticed what I was doing. I was building a case against strangers about carts so I would have somewhere to put a feeling that did not belong to the carts.

I have done this before. I did it with Nina about the shoes. I did it with Mom on the Tuesday call. The shape is the same. Something is sitting in me that I have not let myself look at, and a small thing arrives, and the small thing becomes the shape the sitting thing climbs out through. The carts. The shoes. The pharmacy follow-up. None of them deserved the size of the response, and the response was not really to them.

What was sitting in me, I think — I am working this out as I write, and I am going to try not to tidy it — was the morning. The good morning. The hundred and fifty words on paragraph three. That is the thing that is hard to say, because it sounds backward. The good thing was the thing that had spiked the bad thing. But I think it is true. The morning had given me a small taste of what it would be like to be the woman with the pen behind her ear, on the same side of the table as the thing, and then the morning had ended, and the afternoon had asked me to be a different person — a person who buys milk, a person who picks up an eleven-year-old at four — and the gap between those two people had felt, for one bad minute in a Subaru, unbearable. Unbearable is the wrong word. It was bearable. I bore it. But it had a quality of *this is not fair,* directed at no one, which is the texture of frustration that is out of proportion to what set it off.

What I did in the next few minutes. I sat there for about another minute. I did not get out of the car. I did not call Jamie. I did not do the breathing thing people are supposed to do, because I do not really do the breathing thing. What I did was open the phone, look at the list, and read it slowly. Milk. Eggs. Bread. The cereal Theo eats. Coffee filters. Something for dinner — I had written *something for dinner* on the list, which is funny in retrospect and was not funny in the parking lot. I read the six items twice. The reading slowed me down enough that the fury started to drain, the way it does when you make it look at something specific instead of letting it stay in the abstract. By the time I got to *something for dinner* the second time, I was no longer furious. I was tired. The tired was, I think, what had been underneath the furious the whole time.

I got out of the car. I went into the store. I bought the things on the list, plus a rotisserie chicken for the *something for dinner* slot, plus a small bar of chocolate I did not need and which I ate two squares of in the car before driving to get Nina. I picked up Nina. Nina got in the car with her bag and said *can we get a smoothie,* and I said *not today, kid, I have stuff at home,* in a voice that was, I want to be clear, completely normal. The fury did not come back. Whatever it was had gone where those things go. We drove home. The chicken was fine. The dog was glad to see all of us, including the chicken. I am writing this on Friday morning and I can almost not get back to the size of the feeling I had in that parking lot, which is, I notice, the way these things work — they are total in the moment and faintly embarrassing by the next day.

What I want to put down before the day starts: the spike was not about the milk. The spike was about the morning ending. I should probably know that the morning is going to end. Most mornings end. I am going to try to remember, the next time the spike comes in a parking lot, that the size of it is information about what I had been having, not about what I was being asked to do.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel genuinely at ease around another person — no managing, no editing, no small calculations running in the back of your head? Tell me who you were with and what you were doing, and what it was about them or the setup that let you put the calculations down.
entries.content:
Sunday late morning, on the back step, with Jen.

Jen is the woman down the street with the older lab, the one whose name is actually not Karen — I want to be clear, this is a different person, a third Karen-adjacent woman in this notebook is too many — and she is, technically, a neighbor I have known for eleven years and, functionally, someone I had said maybe forty sentences to in total before this fall. We waved. We did the small things you do at the end of driveways. Then in October the dogs decided, on their own schedule, that they were friends, and the dogs being friends meant Jen and I started ending up on the same loop at roughly the same time of day, and by November the loops had occasionally turned into coffees on her porch or mine. This was, I want to say, the fifth or sixth coffee. Sunday she had texted around ten — *bringing the dog, bringing scones if you have coffee* — and I had said yes without checking anything, and she had walked up the driveway at ten-thirty with the lab and a paper bag, and we had ended up on the back step instead of inside because the sun was on it and the morning was warmer than it should have been for late November.

The dogs sorted themselves out in the yard. The lab is older and has the patience of an older dog, and ours has, in his presence, a slightly performative version of being a younger dog, which she abandons after about three minutes when she realizes he is not going to play and just lies down next to him in the grass with her chin on her paws. They lay there for the entire time Jen was here. Two dogs in a yard in November light.

What I noticed, after Jen left, was that I had not edited a single sentence the whole time she was on the step. Not pre-edited. Not post-edited. I had said things and they had come out and I had not, at any point, run the small grading process on whether they were the right thing to say or whether they had landed or whether the version of me Jen was constructing across from her on a back step was the version I wanted constructed. The grading process, which I now realize runs almost continuously around almost everyone, was off. I do not know when it went off. I think it had been off the whole time.

We talked about — I am trying to reconstruct, and I notice I cannot, fully, which is itself the texture I am trying to describe — the dogs, briefly. A neighbor's tree that had come down in the wind on Friday, which I had not heard about. Her son, who is in college and has a girlfriend Jen has feelings about. A book she had finished and wanted to give me, which she had not brought, which she said she would drop off, and which I notice I believe she actually will. The scones, which were good, and which she had bought at a place I had not been to, and which she described the location of in the slightly imprecise way people describe places they go often. At some point we were quiet for, I want to say, ninety seconds, and the quiet was just quiet. Neither of us scrambled to fill it. The dogs breathed. A car went by on the street. Jen took a sip of her coffee. I took a sip of mine. The quiet ended when she said *oh, I meant to tell you,* about something I have already forgotten.

What it was about her, or about the setup. I have been thinking about this since she left, which was three hours ago, and I think I have a partial answer.

Jen does not know me from before. That is the cleanest piece of it. Jen met me as a woman who walks a dog at nine and lives in the gray house and has two kids she has seen at a distance. Jen does not know what I did for twenty years. Jen has not been told, by me or by anyone else, that I left a job and started a thing. I think she has gathered, from the fact that I am around at irregular hours and from one or two passing sentences on walks, that I work for myself in some configuration, but she has not asked for the shape of it and I have not offered. There is no version of me she is comparing the current me to. There is no parking-lot question waiting in the next sentence. She is not, I am realizing, an audience for the story I have been auditioning. She is just a person on a step.

The other part is that Jen is not, herself, performing. Or if she is I cannot tell, which amounts to the same thing for the purposes of a Sunday morning. She talked about the son's girlfriend with what sounded like the actual mix of fondness and irritation, not the curated version. She said she had been bad at sleeping lately and did not dress it up as a wellness thing or a stress thing, just *I have been bad at sleeping, the dog has noticed, she keeps coming up at three.* When I said something — I think about Theo, something small about him being quiet — she said *yeah, mine got quiet at fourteen too, it comes back,* and did not elaborate, did not turn it into advice, did not make me feel managed. The exchange took maybe four seconds. It cost nothing. It also, I notice now, told me something I had been wanting to know.

What I think the setup gave me was this: Jen and I are operating without a frame. The people I have been most edited around lately are the people who have the most frame on me — Mom, Dani, Pat, Chris, Marcus, the rec center mom. They each have a version of who I am or was or should be, and the editing is the small constant work of meeting the version or refusing it or letting it slide. Jamie has the most frame of all and is also, somehow, the easiest, but with Jamie there is a long marriage doing a lot of the work the frame would otherwise require, and the work is sometimes invisible to me until I am tired. With Jen there is no frame yet. There is a yard, two dogs, scones. Whoever I am on the step is who I am. There is no contradiction to manage.

She left around eleven-forty. She said *same time next week, weather depending,* and I said *yeah, weather depending,* and she walked off down the driveway with the lab. I stayed on the step for another ten minutes after, with the second half of the coffee and the dog at my feet, and noticed, doing a small inventory, that I was not tired. Most social hours leave me tired. This one had not. The grading process is, I am realizing, expensive. The mornings I have been guarding so carefully are partly mornings of not running it. Jen had given me an hour of not running it with another person in the room, which I had not known was available. The dogs were still flat in the grass. The light moved.

entries.prompt: Is there something you've been telling other people you're fine about that you're not actually fine about? Take me into the last time you said the fine version out loud — who you were with, what they'd asked, and what the truer answer would have been.
entries.content:
Wednesday at the dentist, of all places, in the chair while Dr. Halpern's hygienist — Marcy, I think, I have been seeing her for years and I should know — was scaling my lower right and asking me, in the disjointed way they ask you things while their hands are in your mouth, *so are you still doing the consulting thing,* and I said, around the suction, *yeah, still doing it, going great,* and she said *that's wonderful,* and went back to the scaling.

*Going great.* That is what I want to write about.

I had said it without thinking. The sentence had come out clean and warm in the small fraction of a second I had to assemble a sentence around a metal hook in my mouth, and Marcy had taken it and moved on, which was the entire point of the exchange — small talk in the chair is supposed to be a thing both parties dispatch so the actual work can continue — and the *going great* had done its job and had, by the time I was rinsing into the little cup, already started to sit on me funny.

It is not even that *going great* was a lie, exactly. There is a version of the ledger where some things, this month, are going. The doc is open most days. Pat's coffee was good. Karen-down-the-block's sister did call, and we had a conversation that may or may not turn into anything but that did, at least, exist. The hundred and fifty words on paragraph three on the morning of the parking-lot fury are still there, and I added some more on Monday, and the paragraph is, for the first time, a paragraph and not a placeholder. *Going,* in some accounting, yes. *Great,* no. *Great* is the gear-up, the gear that takes a true sentence and gives it a hat. I had reached for the hat without meaning to, the way I reached for the *we* on the call with Daniel. Reflex. The reflex is so old I do not feel myself doing it anymore, and the not-feeling is its own kind of information.

The truer answer, if Marcy could have heard one, was something like: it is going. I do not know if it is going to work. I have made some things this month that I am proud of in a small private way and I have not made the things I am supposed to be making in a public way. I think about going back, sometimes, to a version of what I left, and the thing I left does not exist anymore, which is its own small adjustment. I am tireder than I expected to be. I am also, on certain Tuesdays, more okay than I have been in years. Both. Both at once. *Going* covers the both. *Great* picks one and pretends it is the whole.

But Marcy did not ask for the truer answer. Marcy asked the dental-chair version of the question, which is a greeting, and the *going great* I gave her was the appropriate-sized return on the appropriate-sized question. I am not sure I am bothered by what I said to Marcy. I am bothered by how reflexively it came out, and by how many people I have been giving the *going great* version to without keeping any kind of count.

I have been doing it with Mom, in a different costume. *Slower than I'd like, but I'm working on it,* which I wrote down approvingly in Entry 27 as the one true sentence — true, as far as it goes, but offered with the same managerial intent the *going great* had with Marcy, which is to close the question. I have been doing it with Chris, on the call I finally made and have not properly written about: *figuring out the shape of it, working with a few people, you know how it is,* which Chris, being Chris, did not push on, and which I delivered in the voice that does not invite pushing. I have been doing it with Jamie's brother, with the woman at the coffee place, with the receptionist who books my next dentist appointment — *six months, you'll be back in May, how is everything,* *everything's good, thanks* — with everyone, more or less, who is not on a list of about four people, which list includes Jamie sometimes, Pat sometimes, Jen on Sunday mornings on the step.

The thing I am not actually fine about and have been telling people I am fine about is the timeline. The internal eighteen-month one. Entry 15, the spreadsheet comment, *if revenue starts*, the August me checking in on the November me. We are now closer to ten months than to nine, and the August comment is still deleted, and the timeline is still running in some private corner I have not opened the laptop to look at since. I have not actually told anyone the timeline exists. Not Jamie. Not Mom. Certainly not Marcy. The timeline is the thing under the *going great.* The *going great* is doing the work of keeping the timeline private. If I said the truer sentence — *going, but the clock I gave myself in February is approaching its halfway point, and I do not know if I will have anything to show by it* — the next question would be about the clock, and the clock is mine, and I am not ready to share it, and so I keep saying *going great* and people keep saying *that's wonderful* and the clock keeps ticking in a room nobody else has been told about.

What would have happened if I had said something truer to Marcy. She would have looked at me a beat too long, probably, with the hook still in my mouth, and said *oh, well, hang in there,* and gone back to the scaling, and the appointment would have ended the same way it ended, and I would have been in my car at four-fifteen the same way I was in my car at four-fifteen, and the only difference would have been a small private one — that one more person in my actual life would have a slightly more accurate picture of where I am. Which is, when I write it that way, not a small thing. It is the thing. The accurate pictures are how the not-being-alone-in-it gets built, and I have been, without quite meaning to, declining to build it, one *going great* at a time.

I am not going to start telling Marcy the truer version. The dental chair is not the venue. But I am, I think, going to start noticing the *going great* the way I have started noticing the *we* and the spice cabinet. Noticing is not changing. Noticing is just the part before. The hygienist is named Marcy. I am almost sure. I will check her badge in May.

entries.prompt: There's a kind of compliment you can give yourself that doesn't quite land — where the words are right but something inside doesn't sign off on them. Has that happened recently? Take me to the moment, what you were trying to credit yourself for, and what wouldn't agree.
entries.content:
Tuesday night, around ten, on the couch with the laptop closed and a glass of water I was not really drinking. Jamie was upstairs. The kids were asleep. I had had what was, by any reasonable measure, a good day. I had been at the desk by seven. I had written something on paragraph three that was actually paragraph four — the third one had, on Monday, finally settled, and I had moved past it without ceremony, the way you walk past a door you have been knocking on for months and find it has been unlocked the whole time — and the new paragraph was rougher but it was new, and new is different from rewritten. I had a call at eleven with someone Jen had connected me to, casually, *you should talk to her, she does what you do but in a different field,* and the call had been an hour and had been good, the kind of good where I had taken notes afterward not because I was supposed to but because I wanted to. I had picked Nina up at four. I had made a dinner that everyone ate. The dog had been walked twice.

So on the couch at ten, I tried to give myself the sentence. I tried to say, in my head, *you had a good day. You did good work today.* Plain words. Not even ambitious words. *You did good work today.*

It did not land. I want to describe the not-landing precisely because I have not had a name for this before and I think it is its own thing.

The words went out and something inside, something fast, intercepted them before they could settle. The interception was not loud. It was not the parental voice from earlier entries, the *you are falling off the path* voice, which I know the shape of and have learned to half-recognize. This was quieter. It was something more like a small shrug. *Sure. One day.* Or *yes, but.* The yes was real and the but was the whole sentence. The yes was an accounting concession — fine, we will allow that the day was good — and the but was where the actual position lived, and the actual position was something like *one good day does not constitute evidence, do not try to spend it as evidence, you have spent good days as evidence before and been embarrassed.*

I sat with that for a while. The water in the glass got warmer. The dog, who has started sleeping on the couch as a settled policy, was breathing against my leg.

What I was trying to credit myself for, specifically, was not the day's outputs. The outputs are small and I know they are small. What I was trying to credit myself for was something more like — the showing up. The fact that I had been at the desk by seven on a Tuesday in late November, of my own accord, with no one watching, on a piece of work whose payoff is uncertain and possibly distant, and that I had not, at any point in the morning, opened the spreadsheet or scrolled the industry-adjacent newsletter or otherwise routed around the work. I had just done it. The doing had had a small clean quality. I wanted to give myself the sentence *you are doing the work,* present tense, and I wanted the sentence to land.

It did not. The shrug got there first. *You did the work today. Yesterday you did about forty minutes and called it a day. Last Thursday you did nothing. Do not generalize from a Tuesday.*

The thing I noticed, sitting there, is that the shrug was using the same logic the *not a finisher* story used in Entry 14, but in a more cunning costume. *Not a finisher* was a global verdict that could be argued with by pointing at the twenty-year career, the kids, Pat's framework. The shrug was specific. The shrug was not saying I had never done good work. The shrug was saying *not enough of it, not consistently enough, not in a pattern that would let you say the present-tense sentence yet.* The shrug was, in a way, factually correct on its own terms. I have not, in the last month, worked at the level of Tuesday on most days. Tuesday was a peak, not a floor. The shrug was holding me to the floor and refusing to let me name the peak as anything but a peak.

And yet — this is the part I want to put down — the floor is also higher than it was. I have been at the desk most mornings this month. The doc is open most days. The paragraph that was stuck for twelve weeks is unstuck. The shrug is not measuring those things. The shrug is only measuring whether today's day was sustainable, and answering no, because tomorrow is not yet here to vouch for today.

What would not sign off, when I sit with it, is the part of me that has been burned by self-credit before. I can almost picture her. She is the August me with the cell comment. She is the version who has, more than once, given herself a *you are doing the work* and then watched a week go sideways and felt like a fool for the credit. She is not wrong to be cautious. She has data. The data is that praise issued in advance of pattern is praise that can be retracted, and the retraction is worse than the no-praise would have been. So she withholds. She lets one good day be one good day and refuses to let it become *you are the kind of person who has good days like this.* She is protecting me from a future embarrassment by denying me a present satisfaction. It is a deal I did not consciously make and have apparently been honoring for a long time.

I want to say I argued with her on the couch. I did not, really. I just noticed her. I sat there and let the sentence *you did good work today* be in the room without forcing it to land, and let the shrug be in the room without arguing with it, and after a while I got up and brought the glass to the kitchen and went to bed.

In bed, before sleep, the sentence came back, smaller. *You worked today.* Just that. No *good,* no *the work,* no present continuous. *You worked today.* And that one — I notice — did land. Not as praise. As fact. The shrug had nothing to say to it, because it was not making a claim it could shrug at. It was just reporting.

Maybe that is the size of sentence I can issue myself right now. Not the credit. The report. *You worked today. You walked the dog. You picked up the kid. You called your mother on Sunday and let her go first.* Sentences with no hat on. Things that happened. The credit can come later, when there is a pattern to credit. For now, the report. The shrug cannot do anything with a report. The dog is breathing. The light is off.

entries.prompt: How are you sleeping these days? Pick a night recently when you were awake at an hour you didn't want to be — tell me what time it was, what your body was doing, and what your head kept circling back to.
entries.content:
Last night, two-forty-something. I want to say two-thirty-eight because that is the number my brain insists on, but I am not actually sure I looked, and the looking is the kind of detail my brain manufactures after the fact to make the whole thing feel more like a story than it was. Sometime in the two-thirties or two-forties. Awake. Not the awake where you have to use the bathroom and you can roll over and be back under in ninety seconds. The other awake. The one where you know, the second your eyes open, that the next hour is going to be a transaction.

What my body was doing was the specific configuration I have started to recognize. On my back, which I do not usually sleep on, which means I had rolled there at some point during the bad part of the sleep before the waking. Jaw tight. I have started clenching, apparently — Marcy mentioned it at the cleaning, which I did not write down at the time and which I am writing down now — and the jaw was the first thing I noticed when I came up. Not a headache. Just the held quality, like a fist that has been a fist long enough that opening it is its own small project. My feet were cold. The dog was not on the bed, which she sometimes is now and which I sometimes pretend not to notice; she was downstairs, where I could hear, faintly, the small adjusting sounds she makes in her sleep on the couch she is not supposed to be on.

Jamie was breathing the deep breath. I lay there and did the small accounting you do when you are trying to figure out if you can will yourself back. The accounting said no. The accounting is usually right. I lay there anyway for, I want to say, twenty minutes, doing the thing where you try to be still enough that your body forgets, and my body did not forget, and the head started.

What it circled was not what I expected. I had thought, the few times I have been awake like this lately, that the head would go to Pat's sentence about the room, or to paragraph four which is still soft, or to the call with Jen's friend which I have been replaying in the small repeat-loop way I replay calls that went well, looking for the thing I should have said better. None of those. The head went to a conversation with Theo that had not happened yet and that I do not even know is going to happen. He had come down for water around nine-thirty, before I went up, and had stood at the sink with his back to me and drunk a whole glass and then another one, and had said, into the second glass, *Mom, what do you actually do all day,* and I had said *come on, I told you, I work with people on figuring out career stuff,* and he said *no but, like, what do you actually do,* and I said *I sit at my desk, I make calls, I write,* and he said *cool,* and went back upstairs.

It was a fine exchange. He was not being a snot. He was a fourteen-year-old asking a real question with the small armor that fourteen-year-olds use to ask real questions. I had answered him casually, on the surface, while underneath the answer I had been doing a small fast inventory of what my actual day had looked like, and the inventory had been: about ninety minutes at the desk, two phone calls, a load of laundry, a walk, a call with Mom that was twenty-two minutes, a lot of staring out the window. I had given him the *I sit at my desk, I make calls, I write* version, which is a true sentence in the way *going great* is a true sentence — it covers, it does not lie, it stops the conversation.

What the head was doing at two-forty was constructing the other version of the answer. The one where I had turned around fully and said *you know what, that is a great question, and I am still working out the answer, and I am going to tell you what it actually looks like.* And it kept building the speech in my head, the way the head builds speeches at two in the morning, and I kept noticing that the speech was not for Theo. The speech was for me. I was using a fourteen-year-old at the kitchen sink as the audience for an explanation I had not yet given myself. He had asked the question I have been refusing to ask straight, which is not *what is the work* but *what is the day,* and the day is not the work, and the gap between what I am doing all day and what I think I am supposed to be doing all day is — I notice — the gap that has been waking me up.

I rolled onto my side. The jaw started to let go a little once I was not looking at the ceiling. I did the thing where you try to find a thought that is not the thought, which never works as a strategy and which I keep using anyway. I tried Jen's lab in the grass. I tried the bandana in the dryer. I tried the level on the bracket. They each held for about thirty seconds and then Theo at the sink came back in, with his glass, asking the question, and I was building the speech again.

At some point — three-fifteen, I think, but again I am not sure — I got up. Not to come down, just to the bathroom, and on the way back I stood at the window in the hall for a minute and looked at the street, which was the empty street it is at three-fifteen, with the one light on at the corner that has been on at the corner every night for eleven years. The dog had heard me get up and come halfway up the stairs and stopped, in the way she does when she is asking whether this is a getting-up or a passing-through. I said *go back,* quietly, and she went back. I went back too.

I fell asleep eventually. Not for long. The alarm got me at six-fifteen and I lay in it for ten minutes before I made my feet do the floor. The jaw was still doing its thing. The coffee is on. Theo will come down in about an hour and eat cereal and not ask the question again, because he asked it once and got the answer he was offered, and fourteen-year-olds do not re-ask. The re-asking is mine. The answer is mine to work out, in the day, not in the dark. I am going to try to remember that the next time it is two-forty.

entries.prompt: You mentioned the dog deciding the laundry room is hers, and Marcy noticing the clenching, and Pat's coffee being good — small background figures who keep showing up at the edges. Pick one of them, or someone else who's been on the periphery lately, and tell me about the last actual exchange you had with them.
entries.content:
The actual exchange with Marcy was about three minutes long and I have been turning it over since.

I had been in the chair maybe fifteen minutes. The scaling was done, the polishing was done, she had taken the bib off and was in the small post-cleaning configuration where she is mostly clicking things into the computer and you are mostly sitting up trying to find your purse with your eyes. She had her back to me at the keyboard. I was running my tongue over my teeth the way you do, doing the small private audit of cleanness, and she said, without turning around, *so I want to mention something I noticed today.* Pleasant voice. The voice of a person who has had this conversation many times.

What she had noticed was the wear on the lower right. She turned around then, and she did the thing where she held up the small mirror and pointed with the end of a pen, and showed me a place on a back molar where the surface was, apparently, flatter than it should be. *That's grinding,* she said. *Or clenching. Probably clenching, actually, the pattern is more clench than grind.* She said the pattern was new since May. She had the May notes up on the screen.

I said *huh.* I said it in the voice that is acknowledging information without committing to a feeling about it. I said *I guess that tracks.*

She said, still pleasant, *are you stressed.*

That was the question. *Are you stressed.* In the dental chair, at three-fifteen on a Wednesday, from a woman whose last name I am not sure I have ever known, holding a small pen.

What I said was *not particularly, no more than usual,* which was — I want to be accurate — the *going great* of stress. Reflex. Came out before I could grade it. Marcy nodded the way she nods, which is a nod that does not require anything of the nodder, and said, *well, the night guard is something to think about. Insurance covers most of it. You wouldn't have to decide today.* And she clicked back to the computer and printed out the slip with my next appointment on it, and that was the end of it.

What I have been turning over is not the night guard, which I will probably get, the way I get things eventually after letting the recommendation sit for two visits. What I have been turning over is *no more than usual.*

*No more than usual.* Said to a person who has, by her own data, been looking at the inside of my mouth twice a year for going on a decade. She has, in some technical sense, more longitudinal information about my body than almost anyone. She had the May notes. She had the November tooth. She had told me, in a sentence, that something measurable about me had changed since May, and I had answered with a sentence that said nothing measurable had changed. The two sentences were, on their face, in disagreement, and she had let mine stand without contesting it, because she is a hygienist and not my mother and her job is to mention the night guard, not to interrogate the patient about whether the patient is being honest with herself in the chair.

I left. I paid at the desk. I sat in the car in the parking lot — different parking lot, different fury, no fury this time, just the post-dentist tongue-feel and the specific quality of three-fifteen light in November — and I thought *huh* in a different register than I had said it inside. I have been clenching in my sleep since May. I left the firm in February. The math on that was not subtle. May is when the *figuring out the shape of it* phase, the early-excited phase, the phase where I was still riding the small adrenaline of having done a brave thing, started to soften into the actual phase, the one where the bravery was behind me and the building was in front of me and the building was slow. May was, I think, when the spreadsheet visits at eleven started. May was around when I rewrote the top of the doc the first time. The body had apparently started clenching around the same time the brain started rewriting paragraph two, and neither of them had let me in on it.

I have not told Jamie about the tooth. That is now four things, by my count, that I have not told Jamie — the boots, the marsh morning eventually told, Marcus eventually mentioned in the breezy version, and now the tooth — and I am going to tell Jamie this one, I think, tonight, because the night guard is going to arrive in the mail at some point and Jamie is going to ask, and because the tooth is small enough to put on the table without it becoming the whole evening. *I have apparently been clenching. Marcy noticed. I am getting the thing for it.* Three sentences. Jamie will say *huh,* and probably *since when,* and I will say *since May, apparently,* and Jamie will do the math the same way I did it in the parking lot and not say it, because Jamie is good at not saying it, and we will move on to whether Theo needs new shoes.

What I keep coming back to is that Marcy had data on me I did not have on me. She had a back molar from May and a back molar from November and the difference between them, and she gave me the difference, in the kind voice, with the small pen, and I gave her *no more than usual* in return. The exchange is sitting on me funny because I think it is the cleanest example yet of the gap I have been writing around. There is a version of me that is being recorded by the world — by Marcy's notes, by Jamie's *easier to be around*, by Theo's question at the sink, by the fact that Karen down the block thought of me when her sister was asking — and that version is more accurate, in places, than the version I am narrating from inside. The world keeps offering me corrections. I keep saying *no more than usual* and taking the slip with the next appointment on it.

May. The night guard. The tooth. I am going to drink water before bed tonight, which is a thing the dentist always tells you and which I never do. Small report. No hat.

entries.prompt: Who have you been thinking about more than you've been in touch with? Tell me about the last time they crossed your mind — where you were, what brought them up, and what you did or didn't do with the thought.
entries.content:
Dad, mostly. I want to put that down at the top because I have been circling it for two days and the circling itself is the thing.

Sunday afternoon, in the basement, looking for the small box of holiday lights that I knew was on the metal shelving unit by the furnace and that turned out, when I got down there, to be on a different shelving unit, behind two other boxes, in a configuration I am pretty sure Dad set up the last time he came over to help us with something, which would have been — I am doing the math while I am writing — eight or nine years ago. Maybe more. Long enough that I cannot pinpoint the visit. He used to come up and do the small jobs around the house that I could do but would not get to, and we would not call them visits because calling them visits would have required acknowledging that he was driving an hour to do work I had not asked him to do, so we called them dropping by, and he dropped by, and the basement got organized in his organizing logic, which was not mine and which I have been living inside of ever since.

The shelving had his label-maker labels on it. *XMAS,* with the periods. *TAX 2014–.* *CAMPING.* We have not been camping as a family since before Nina. The labels are from a man who believed, reasonably, that the labels would last longer than his use of them, and he was right, and I was standing in front of them on Sunday afternoon, looking for lights, and I had to sit down on the bottom step for a minute because the labels did the thing the labels do.

He has been gone six years in March. I know the math without doing it. The grief has long since done the thing grief does, which is move from a weather system to a feature of the landscape, and I do not, most days, miss him the way I missed him at one year or two. I miss him the way you miss a particular kind of light. It comes in slant, around objects.

What brought him up on Sunday was the labels and also, I think, Theo. I had asked Theo to come down and help me carry the boxes up, and he had come down, in the way fourteen-year-olds come down which is half a minute later than they could have, and he had read the labels too. He said *who wrote these,* in the voice that is curious without committing to caring, and I said *Grandpa,* and he said *huh,* and picked up the lights box, and went back upstairs. That was the whole exchange. Twelve seconds, maybe. But Theo is fourteen, which is the age I was when I started having actual conversations with my father, the kind that were not just the small parental call-and-response but the kind where he said something and I had to think about it for a week, and I stood in the basement with my hand on the *XMAS* label and felt the small specific gap between Theo at fourteen with a grandfather who is a label-maker artifact and me at fourteen with a father who was, at the time, exactly Theo's grandfather's age. Roughly. The math is rough. The shape is right.

What I did with the thought was carry the lights up and not say anything to Theo about it. I have been doing this with Dad since he died — letting him come up and not bringing him into the room — and I am not sure why. Some of it is that the kids did not know him well enough for him to be a shared figure. Theo was eight when he died, Nina five. They have a few memories each, most of which are probably actually memories of photographs of memories, which is how it goes. To talk about Dad with them now would be to introduce a person, not to refer to one, and the introducing has felt like more work than I have had the appetite for, and so the not-introducing has accumulated into a kind of policy, and the policy is now six years old.

The person I have been thinking about and not been in touch with is Dad, in the sense that you can be in touch with someone who is dead, by which I mean: I have not been talking to him in my head, the way I did the first couple of years, when I would catch myself narrating things to him while doing dishes. The narration had stopped at some point — I cannot tell you when — and I had not noticed the stopping until Sunday in the basement, when the labels reopened the channel and I realized the channel had been closed.

What he would have made of this nine months I do not actually know. I want to write the version where he would have been on my side, where he would have said *give it the time it takes, you know what you're doing,* but I am not sure that is the version. He was a man who held a job at the same place for thirty-one years and retired with a small ceremony and a clock. The clock is in our hallway, ticking, which I notice now I have made into a small running joke about how literal he could be and which is also, on a Sunday afternoon in a basement with his labels, not a joke. He might have been the one asking *are things picking up,* in his own costume, with more love in it than Mom puts in but the same underlying question. I do not know. I cannot ask him. The not-being-able-to-ask is a different shape than the not-calling-Chris shape, and I have been treating them as the same shape for years, which I am noticing now is wrong.

I came back upstairs with the second box. Theo had already gone to his room. The lights got hung on Monday. Jamie did not ask why I had been quiet at dinner Sunday and I did not offer. Some people you keep in touch with by lighting the house they helped wire the basement of, which is a sentence I would not say out loud and which is what happened. The clock in the hallway is still ticking. I have not wound it in a while. I am going to wind it tonight.

entries.prompt: What's something you've spent money on recently — or thought hard about spending money on — where the deciding wasn't really about the money? Take me into the moment you were weighing it, where you were and what was in front of you.
entries.content:
Tuesday afternoon, at the desk, looking at the registration page for a thing — I am going to call it a thing because naming it would make it more real than I want it to be on this page — that runs three days in February in a city I would have to fly to. Twenty-two hundred dollars, plus the flight, plus the hotel which they have negotiated at a discount and which is still two-fifty a night for three nights, plus the meals and the airport coffee and the small bookkeeping of being away from the family for a long weekend, which has its own cost in the form of Jamie running the show alone and the kids missing whatever they were going to need someone for. Call it three thousand, all in. I had the tab open and I was looking at the photo at the top of the page, which was a photo of about forty people in a room with name tags on, smiling at someone off-camera, and I was, I want to be honest, kind of hating the photo while also wanting to be in it.

The deciding was not about the three thousand. We have three thousand. The spreadsheet at eleven would not have changed in a way I cannot already predict. The careful budget would absorb three thousand the way the careful budget absorbs most things, which is by tightening somewhere else for a few weeks without me having to explicitly identify the somewhere. The money was not the question. The money was the place I was pretending the question was, while a different question sat next to me on the desk.

The different question was about who I would be in the room.

Jen had sent the link. That is part of the texture. Jen, who is not in my field and does not know the field, had been at a dinner where someone had mentioned the thing, and Jen had thought of me in the way Karen down the block had thought of me, which is something I am still learning to take in as a category of event — neighbors thinking of me when adjacent things come up — and had texted *no idea if this is for you, but it sounded like the kind of thing you said you go to.* I had not said I go to things. I had, in some recent conversation, said I had been thinking about going to things, in the breezy voice, in the *going great* register, and Jen had filed it. So the link arrived with Jen attached to it, which meant the link arrived with a small accountability I would not have given it from a stranger.

I clicked through. I read the agenda. The agenda was — I am trying to be fair — interesting. Real people whose work I have read. Sessions with shapes I could imagine being in. A track that lined up, in a way I noticed before I let myself notice it, with what Pat said about the room being the thing. There was a session on practice-building specifically for people in the messy middle, which is a phrase I rolled my eyes at and then registered as accurate and then rolled my eyes at again. The thing was not a scam. The thing was a real thing that real people would be at, doing the kind of work I am, on most days, trying to do.

What was in front of me was the registration page. What was running was the audit.

The audit said: you do not have enough to show. You will go to this thing and you will be in a name-tag photo and people will ask you what you do and you will say *figuring out the shape of it* and you will be in that room as a person figuring out the shape of it, and the three thousand will have bought you the experience of being underdressed for your own profession. The audit was loud. The audit had specifics. It rehearsed the dinner where I would not know whether to sit at the table with the people who looked like they were in their own version of paragraph four, or with the people who looked like they had it together, both of which would cost me something, and there would not be a third table.

The other voice — quieter, and I do not entirely trust it yet, which is its own kind of information — said: the reason to go is that you do not have enough to show. The reason to go is exactly that. You go to the room before you have the thing because the room is partly how the thing comes. You go and you sit at the wrong table and you have the awkward conversation and you come home with three names and an idea and a small recalibration, and the three thousand bought you the recalibration. People who wait until they have enough to show are people who never go.

I did not register. I closed the tab. Then I opened it again about an hour later and read the agenda one more time and closed it again. Then in the evening, after dinner, I opened it a third time on the laptop on the couch, and Jamie saw the page from across the room and said, neutrally, *what's that,* and I said *something Jen sent me, in February,* and Jamie said *are you going,* and I said *I don't know yet,* and Jamie said *you should go if you want to go,* in the voice that is not pushing and is also, somehow, pushing, and I closed the tab a third time and have not opened it since.

What I am sitting with is that the three thousand was the costume the question was wearing. The question was: do I get to count myself as the kind of person who goes to this kind of thing, in the current configuration. Not someday, when there is a roster and a card. Now. With the doc still on paragraph four. With *no more than usual* on the dental record. The audit's answer is no, not yet, do not embarrass yourself, save the three thousand for when you have earned the room. The other voice's answer is that the earning is partly what the room is for.

I notice I am not going to decide tonight. I notice that the boots, in their tab at ten p.m., I clicked. I notice that this one I keep not clicking. The boots were finishable. The room is not. The room is a bet on a version of me that is not yet visible, including to me, and I have been declining to place that bet in a lot of small ways for a lot of months, and the deadline to register is three weeks out, and I am going to sit with the tab closed and see what is true in a week. Jamie is right that I should go if I want to go. The if is the whole sentence. The dog is asleep. The kettle is on for the second cup, which I am going to be honest about in the report at the end of the day.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel proud of someone in your life — your kid, a friend, a coworker, anyone? Take me into the moment it hit you. What were they doing, and what was the pride actually about?
entries.content:
Theo, Friday night, in the kitchen, around eight. He had been at a friend's house for the afternoon and had walked home in the dark, which is new — the walking home alone after dark, not the friend's house — and he had come in through the garage and put his shoes by the door instead of in the middle of the floor, which is also new, and had gone to the fridge and stood in front of it the way Nina stands in front of it, scanning, which is a posture I had not realized he had inherited until I saw it from the side.

I was at the table with the laptop closed and a cup of tea, the decaf-at-night thing I have apparently fully committed to, and Jamie was upstairs with Nina helping with something for school. Theo did the fridge thing, came up empty, closed the door, and then — without sitting down, leaning against the counter with one foot on the other — said, *hey, can I ask you something.*

I said *yeah, of course.*

He said, *Aiden's parents are getting divorced. He told me today. I didn't know what to say.*

I want to write this carefully because what happened next is what I am writing about, and the thing I am writing about is small.

I did the small adult thing of not jumping. I did not say *oh no.* I did not ask the questions I wanted to ask, which were all about Aiden's mother who I have known a little for years and who is, I would have guessed, the leaving party though I do not know that and have no business guessing it. I said *that's a hard thing for him to tell you. What did you do.*

Theo said, *I just kind of said that sucks. And then I didn't know if that was the right thing. So I asked him if he wanted to play the game or if he wanted to not play the game and just hang out, and he said the game, and we played the game for like two hours and didn't talk about it. And then when I left he said thanks for not being weird about it.*

He said this looking at the floor mostly, with the small embarrassment of a fourteen-year-old reporting on something that mattered to him, the embarrassment that is not really embarrassment but more like a person showing you a thing they made and not being sure if you are going to handle it carefully.

I said, *that sounds like exactly the right thing to do.*

He said, *yeah?*

I said, *yeah. You gave him the choice. He took the one he needed. You didn't make him perform being okay and you didn't make him perform being not-okay. That's a real thing to know how to do at fourteen.*

He shrugged, which is what fourteen-year-olds do with sentences like that, but he did not move away from the counter. He stayed for another minute, fiddling with the edge of the dish towel that was hanging on the oven handle. Then he said, *okay, well, I'm gonna go up,* and went up. The shoes stayed by the door.

I sat there with the tea for a long time after.

What I want to put down is what the pride was actually about, because I notice the obvious answer — *my son is kind* — is true and is also not the thing. The thing is more specific. The thing is that he had read the room. He had been handed a difficult sentence by a friend and he had not, in the moment, reached for a script. He had not done the *going great* of grief, which would have been *oh man, that's awful, are you okay, do you want to talk about it,* the over-attentive version that would have made Aiden into a project. He had not done the deflection version either, which would have been to change the subject and pretend nothing had been said. He had done the third thing, which is the one almost nobody can do at fourteen and which a lot of people cannot do at forty-seven, which is to acknowledge the thing and then offer the friend a choice about what kind of company they wanted in the next two hours. He had let Aiden run the room. He had been the room.

I sat at the table and felt, for a minute, slightly out of breath. I want to write this part down because it is the part I keep wanting to look away from. The pride was not separate from a small private rearrangement. The kid who I have been worried about for being quiet, whose absence I was getting used to and writing about as a developmental thing we would maybe see the other side of at twenty — that kid had been, while I was not looking, becoming a person I would want to be a friend to. He had been doing it without me. He had not learned it from a sentence I had given him. I do not even know where he learned it. From watching, presumably. From watching us, sometimes, doing the thing right and sometimes doing it wrong. From watching his own friends. From the small accumulation of being a fourteen-year-old who pays attention.

The pride was about him, and about the kindness, and it was also — I am going to be honest — about the small evidence the moment provided that the worrying had been a worrying and not a verdict. Theo at the counter with the dish towel was a Theo I had not known was in there, and he had been in there the whole time. The not-knowing was on me. He had been composing himself out of view, the way he had been composing the impressions in social studies, and I had been filing reports on his absence while he was, in fact, present somewhere I could not see.

I did not tell Jamie that night. I want to tell Jamie tonight. I want to say it small, the way Jamie said the easier-to-be-around sentence, without making it a story we have to handle. *Theo did a thing today. I want to tell you about it.* And then say it. And then let it be in the room.

Pat would say I was better in the room with him in that minute than I have been in any room I have been auditioning for. I notice I think Pat would be right. The tea is cold. Theo's shoes are still by the door. I am not going to move them.

entries.prompt: When did you last feel genuinely caught off guard by something someone said — in a good way or a bad one? Take me into the second right after the words landed, before you'd worked out your response.
entries.content:
Wednesday, late morning, on the phone with Karen-down-the-block's sister, whose name is Lauren and who I have now talked to twice. The first call, two weeks ago, was the introductory one, the are-we-a-fit one, and it had gone fine in the way those calls go fine — she had told me about her situation, I had asked the questions I have learned to ask, we had agreed to talk again. This was the again. I had taken it at the desk, headset on, the doc closed because I have started closing it during calls instead of leaving it open behind, which is a small thing I am giving myself credit for in the no-hat way.

We were maybe thirty minutes in. She had been telling me about a conversation with her boss that had gone sideways, and I had been doing the thing I do, which is mostly listening and occasionally saying the small sentence that opens the next paragraph instead of closing it. The work, when it is working, is not complicated. It is mostly making room.

And then she said, in the middle of a sentence that was about something else, *you know, I was kind of nervous to call you. My sister said you used to be a big deal at — at the place you were before. I almost didn't reach out because I figured you'd be too — I don't know. Polished. But you're not. You're, like, actually listening. It's been really helpful.*

She kept talking. She moved on to the next thing about the boss. She did not, I want to be clear, mean the sentence as a compliment in the way compliments usually arrive, framed and presented. She had said it the way you say a thing that is on your way to a different thing. By the time I caught up, she was two sentences past it.

What happened in the second after the words landed — the actual second, before I had worked out anything — was that I lost the thread of what she was saying. I did not lose it for long. Maybe four seconds, maybe six. But for those seconds I was not in the call. I was somewhere behind my own face. The headset was still on. Lauren was still talking. And the inside of me had gone briefly silent in a specific way, the way a room goes silent when somebody opens a window you did not know was a window.

*You're not. You're, like, actually listening.*

I want to be accurate about what was in the silent second. It was not pleasure, exactly, though pleasure was in there somewhere underneath. It was more like — being seen sideways. She had not been looking at me. She had been looking at her own situation, and she had reported, in passing, what I looked like from where she was sitting. And what I looked like was: not polished. Actually listening. Those are two descriptions, and I had been bracing, without knowing I was bracing, for the first one. I had assumed, going into the call, that the version of me Lauren was meeting was the version with the bookshelf behind it, the *we*, the costume from the Daniel call. I had been working, in some half-conscious way, to perform a smaller version of that costume — quieter, more humble, but still the costume — because I thought the costume was what made the room feel like a session and not a chat. Lauren had just told me the costume had not arrived. She was meeting a person, and the person was the work.

The other thing in the silent second was a small hot embarrassment, which I am writing down because it was real. *Used to be a big deal.* Karen had told her sister that.
