Karen, who I had thought was operating on the *Riley does this now* read, had also been operating on a *Riley used to be that* read, and had passed both to her sister, and the sister had walked into our first call with a picture of me that included a past tense I have not been carrying around in front of myself. *Used to be.* The phrase was correct. It was also vertiginous, the way Marcus's admiration had been vertiginous in the cereal aisle, but in the opposite direction. Marcus's admiration had been built on a future tense that was not yet true. Lauren's nervousness had been built on a past tense that was no longer true. Both of them were operating on tenses I do not currently occupy. Both of them had, in the meeting, found me in the present tense, and the present tense had — apparently — been enough.

I came back into the call. I do not think Lauren noticed the four or six seconds; she was mid-sentence, and I picked up the thread on the boss with a small *and what did you say back to that,* which is a question I can ask in my sleep. The call kept going. We made it to the end. She said she wanted to schedule the next one. I said yes, picked a time, hung up.

Then I sat at the desk for a long minute, headset still on my head, before I took it off.

What kept coming back, while I sat there, was not the *actually listening.* It was the *I almost didn't reach out.* She had almost not called. The polished version of me, the one that has been the thing I have been failing to construct for nine months at the desk, is the version that would have lost me Lauren before she dialed. The thing I have been treating as the obstacle to having clients — not having a website, not having a card, not having the costume — is, in this one specific instance, the thing that let one come through. She came because the costume had not been built yet. If I had managed, in August or September, to put up the polished version of the page and the bio and the framework, Karen would have looked at it and Lauren would have not called.

I do not want to overread this. One person. One *almost didn't.* I have been overreading smaller things. But I want to put it on the page next to Pat's *better in the room,* next to Jen's *no frame,* next to the version of me Theo handled a hard moment near without performing for, and notice that the data, when I let it be data, keeps pointing in the same direction. It is not the direction I was pointing. It is a direction. Four people, four different rooms, the same arrow.

I took the headset off. I did not open the doc. I made a fresh coffee — third, but it is a Wednesday — and I stood at the kitchen window for a minute and watched a different squirrel do a different thing with the bird feeder. The arrow is still on the desk. I am going to let it sit there overnight before I do anything with it. I am also going to text Karen, later, just *thank you for thinking of me with Lauren.* No more than that. The small report. No hat.

parts.name: The Auditor

parts.name: The Avoider
parts.description: Distracts, delays, withdraws, and routes around discomfort — closes laptops, walks dogs, watches baking shows, leaves things on counters.

parts.name: The Caretaker
parts.description: Brings tender, accurate presence to other people — patient with Mom, kind to Nina, attentive to Theo — even while withholding the same from herself.

parts.name: The Curator
parts.description: Performs polish and competence, reaches for costumes and props to manage how she's perceived.

parts.name: The Frightened One
parts.description: Carries the scared, exposed, fraudulent feeling underneath — the small humiliated self who fears she has stepped off and won't get back on.

parts.name: The Smoother
parts.description: Says the warm sentence that closes the room — "going great," "that'll be nice" — to protect everyone from a truer answer that would cost more.

parts.name: The Surveillance Worrier
parts.description: Tracks ledgers, runs comparisons, checks spreadsheets, and pre-disappoints to hedge against a future verdict.
